State in East Malaysia

**Sarawak** ([/səˈrɑːwɒk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*sə-RAH-wok*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Malay: [[saˈrawaʔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malay "Help:IPA/Malay")) is a [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia")[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). It is the largest of the 13 states, with an area almost equal to that of [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia"). Sarawak is in [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia"), in northwest [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), and is bordered by the Malaysian state of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") to the northeast, [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan") (the [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") portion of Borneo) to the south, and [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") to the north. The state capital, [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching"), is Sarawak's largest city, its economic centre, and the seat of the state government. Other cities and towns in Sarawak include [Miri](/wiki/Miri,_Malaysia "Miri, Malaysia"), [Sibu](/wiki/Sibu "Sibu"), and [Bintulu](/wiki/Bintulu "Bintulu"). As of the 2020 Malaysia census, Sarawak's population was 2.453 million.[[9]](#cite_note-Dayak_Daily-9) Sarawak has an [equatorial climate](/wiki/Equatorial_climate "Equatorial climate"), with tropical [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") and abundant animal and plant species. It has several prominent [cave](/wiki/Cave "Cave") systems at [Gunung Mulu National Park](/wiki/Gunung_Mulu_National_Park "Gunung Mulu National Park"). [Rajang River](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River") is Malaysia's longest river; [Bakun Dam](/wiki/Bakun_Dam "Bakun Dam"), one of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")'s largest dams, is on one of its tributaries, the [Balui River](/wiki/Balui_River "Balui River"). [Mount Murud](/wiki/Mount_Murud "Mount Murud") is Sarawak's highest point. Sarawak is Malaysia's only state with a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Malaysia "Christianity in Malaysia") majority.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The earliest known human settlement in Sarawak at the [Niah Caves](/wiki/Niah_National_Park "Niah National Park") dates back 40,000 years. A series of Chinese ceramics dated from the 8th to 13th century AD was uncovered at the archaeological site of Santubong. Sarawak's coastal regions came under the influence of the [Bruneian Empire](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") in the 16th century. In 1839, [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"), a British explorer, arrived in Sarawak. He and his descendants [governed](/wiki/Raj_of_Sarawak "Raj of Sarawak") the state from 1841 to 1946. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was [occupied by the Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_British_Borneo "Japanese occupation of British Borneo") for three years. After the war, the last [White Rajah](/wiki/White_Rajah "White Rajah"), [Charles Vyner Brooke](/wiki/Charles_Vyner_Brooke "Charles Vyner Brooke"), ceded Sarawak to Britain, and in 1946 it became a [British Crown Colony](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak"). The British granted Sarawak self-government on 22 July 1963 and it subsequently became one of Malaysia's founding members, but Indonesia opposed the federation, leading to a three-year [confrontation](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation"). Malaysia's creation also prompted a [communist insurgency](/wiki/Communist_insurgency_in_Sarawak "Communist insurgency in Sarawak") that lasted until 1990.

The head of state is the governor, also known as the [Yang di-Pertua Negeri](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri_Sarawak "Yang di-Pertua Negeri Sarawak"), and the head of government is the [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Sarawak "Premier of Sarawak"). Sarawak is divided into administrative divisions and districts, governed by a system closely modelled on the [Westminster parliamentary system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), Malaysia's earliest state legislature system. Under the Malaysian constitution, Sarawak has greater autonomy than the states in Peninsular Malaysia.

Because of its natural resources, Sarawak specialises in the export of oil and gas, [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber") and [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil"), but also has strong manufacturing, energy and tourism sectors. It is ethnically, culturally, religiously and linguistically diverse; its ethnic groups include Iban, Chinese, Malay, Bidayuh, Melanau, Orang Ulu, Indian, Eurasian and Kedayan.[[22]](#cite_note-22) English and Malay are the state languages;[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) there is no official religion.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![A black bird with white underbelly, white beak and red-orange horn perching on a branch](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Buceros_rhinoceros_-Kuala_Lumpur_Bird_Park%2C_Malaysia-8a_%281%29.jpg/250px-Buceros_rhinoceros_-Kuala_Lumpur_Bird_Park%2C_Malaysia-8a_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buceros_rhinoceros_-Kuala_Lumpur_Bird_Park,_Malaysia-8a_(1).jpg)

The [rhinoceros hornbill](/wiki/Rhinoceros_hornbill "Rhinoceros hornbill") is the [state bird](/wiki/State_emblems_of_Malaysia "State emblems of Malaysia") of Sarawak.

The generally accepted explanation of the state's name is that it derives from the [Sarawak Malay](/wiki/Sarawak_Malay "Sarawak Malay") word *Serawak*, which means [antimony](/wiki/Antimony "Antimony").[[27]](#cite_note-etymology-27) A popular alternative explanation is that it is a contraction of the four Malay words Pangeran Muda Hashim (the Sultan of Brunei's uncle) purportedly uttered when he gave Sarawak to [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"), an English explorer, in 1841: "*Saya serah pada awak*" ("I surrender it to you").[[27]](#cite_note-etymology-27) But that is false: the territory was named Sarawak before Brooke arrived, and the word *awak* was not in Sarawak Malay's vocabulary before the formation of Malaysia.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The area of today's Sarawak was known as **Cerava** by Portuguese cartographers in the 16th century.

Sarawak is nicknamed "Land of the Hornbills" (*Bumi Kenyalang*). These birds are important cultural symbols for the Dayak people, representing the spirit of God. It is believed that if a hornbill is seen flying over residences, it will bring the local community good luck. Sarawak has eight of the world's 54 species of hornbills, and the [Rhinoceros hornbill](/wiki/Rhinoceros_hornbill "Rhinoceros hornbill") is Sarawak's state bird.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Sarawak](/wiki/History_of_Sarawak "History of Sarawak")

Foragers are known to have lived around the west mouth of the [Niah Caves](/wiki/Niah_Caves "Niah Caves") (located 110 km (68 mi) southwest of [Miri](/wiki/Miri,_Malaysia "Miri, Malaysia")) 40,000 years ago.[[30]](#cite_note-Sarawakforestry-30)[[31]](#cite_note-worldarcheology-31) A modern human skull found near the Niah Caves is the oldest example of human remains found in Malaysia and the oldest modern human skull from Southeast Asia.[[30]](#cite_note-Sarawakforestry-30)[[31]](#cite_note-worldarcheology-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Chinese ceramics dating to the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") and [Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") dynasties (8th to 13th century AD, respectively) found at Santubong (near [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching")) hint at its significance as a seaport.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

One of the earliest Chinese records of an independent kingdom in Borneo is the 977 AD letter to the Chinese emperor from the ruler of Boni, which some scholars believe to refer to Borneo.[[35]](#cite_note-Hutton2000-35) The Bruneians regained their independence from Srivijaya due to the onset of a Javanese-Sumatran war.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1225, the Chinese official [Zhao Rukuo](/wiki/Zhao_Rukuo "Zhao Rukuo") reported that Boni had 100 warships to protect its trade, and that there was great wealth in the kingdom.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") suggested in his memoirs that the Great Khan or the ruler of the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire"), attempted and failed many times in invading "Great Java" which was the European name for Bruneian controlled Borneo.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In the 1300s the Chinese annals, *Nanhai zhi*, reported that Brunei invaded or administered Sarawak and [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") as well as the Philippine kingdoms of [Butuan](/wiki/Rajahnate_of_Butuan "Rajahnate of Butuan"), [Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu"), [Ma-i](/wiki/Ma-i "Ma-i") (Mindoro), Malilu 麻裏蘆 (present-day [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila")), [Shahuchong](/wiki/Sanmalan "Sanmalan") 沙胡重 (present-day Siocon), Yachen 啞陳 ([Oton](/wiki/Oton "Oton")), and 文杜陵 Wenduling (present-day [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao")),[[42]](#cite_note-42) which would regain their independence at a later date.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The Melano kingdom existed from about 1300 to 1400 AD and was centred at the Mukah River. Rajah Tugau was a King in Sarawak who was mentioned in both [Bruneian](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei")[[44]](#cite_note-PreislamicBruneiKings-44) and Filipino ([Madja-as](/wiki/Madja-as "Madja-as")) sources.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) His kingdom covered coastal Sarawak until Belait and consisted of groups of similar Melanau and Kajang language speakers who shared an almost identical culture and heritage. *Nagarakertagama*, written in 1365 during Hayam Wuruk, mentions Malano and Barune(ng) among the 14 tributaries of Majapahit. After the fall of Majapahit, Barune(ng) expanded its territory along the northern coast of Borneo.[[47]](#cite_note-Nicholl-47): 36 According to the manuscript of Brunei's rulers, following the fall of Majapahit, Barunai led by Awang Semaun being reinforced by the Iban, conquered Tutong under its chief Mawanga and the whole of the Melano kingdom until Igan under its chief Basiung despite reinforcement from Sambas. Barunai continued the conquest of the entire south and then north of Borneo, after which he conquered the whole of Sulu and the Philippines.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In the 14th century, the [Javanese](/wiki/Java "Java") manuscript *[Nagarakretagama](/wiki/Nagarakretagama "Nagarakretagama")*, written by [Prapanca](/wiki/Mpu_Prapanca "Mpu Prapanca") in 1365, mentioned *Barune* as the constituent state of Hindu [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit"),[[49]](#cite_note-49) which had to make an annual [tribute](/wiki/Tribute "Tribute") of forty [katis](/wiki/Catty "Catty") of [camphor](/wiki/Camphor "Camphor"). In 1369, Sulu which was also formerly part of Majapahit, successfully rebelled and then attacked Boni, and had invaded the Northeast Coast of Borneo[[50]](#cite_note-50) and afterwards had looted the capital of its treasure and gold including sacking two sacred pearls. A fleet from Majapahit succeeded in driving away the Sulus, but Boni was left weaker after the attack.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200944-51) A Chinese report from 1371 described Boni as poor and totally controlled by Majapahit.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200945-52)

The [Bruneian Empire](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") was established in the coastal regions of Sarawak by the mid-15th century,[[53]](#cite_note-53) and the Kuching area was known to Portuguese [cartographers](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") during the 16th century as *Cerava*, one of the five great seaports of Borneo.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) The [Sultanate of Sarawak](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sarawak "Sultanate of Sarawak") was established during this time and lasted for almost half a century before being reunited with Brunei in 1641.[[56]](#cite_note-srwk-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) By the early 19th century, the Bruneian Empire was in decline, retaining only a tenuous hold on the coastal regions of Sarawak which were otherwise controlled by semi-independent [Malay](/wiki/Ethnic_Malays "Ethnic Malays") leaders. Away from the coast, territorial wars were fought between the [Iban](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people") and a [Kenyah](/wiki/Kenyah_people "Kenyah people")-[Kayan](/wiki/Kayan_people_(Borneo) "Kayan people (Borneo)") alliance.[[58]](#cite_note-Fairlandsarawak-58)

[![Brooke painted by Francis Grant, standing next to a rock in front of a tropical landscape](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Sir_James_Brooke_%281847%29_by_Francis_Grant.jpg/250px-Sir_James_Brooke_%281847%29_by_Francis_Grant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_James_Brooke_(1847)_by_Francis_Grant.jpg)

[James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"), the first [White Rajah](/wiki/White_Rajahs "White Rajahs")

The discovery of antimony ore in the Kuching region led [Pengiran Indera Mahkota](/wiki/Pengiran_Indera_Mahkota "Pengiran Indera Mahkota"), a representative of the Sultan of Brunei, to increase development in the territory between 1824 and 1830. Increasing antimony production in the region led the Brunei Sultanate to demand higher taxes, which ultimately led to civil unrest.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In 1839, Sultan [Omar Ali Saifuddin II](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_II "Omar Ali Saifuddin II") (1827–1852) assigned his uncle [Pengiran Muda Hashim](/wiki/Pengiran_Muda_Hashim "Pengiran Muda Hashim") the task of restoring order but his inability to do so caused him to request the aid of British sailor [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"). Brooke's success in quelling the revolt was rewarded with antimony, property and the governorship of Sarawak, which at that time consisted only of a small area centred on Kuching.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The [Brooke family](/wiki/Brooke_family "Brooke family"), later called the [White Rajahs](/wiki/White_Rajahs "White Rajahs"), set about expanding the territory they had been ceded.[[62]](#cite_note-62) With expansion came the need for efficient governance and thus, beginning in 1841, Sarawak was separated into the first of its administrative divisions[[63]](#cite_note-The_Borneo_Post-63) and the [Sarawak dollar](/wiki/Sarawak_dollar "Sarawak dollar"), entered circulation in 1858.[[64]](#cite_note-64) By 1912, a total of five divisions had been established in Sarawak, each headed by a [Resident](/wiki/Resident_(title) "Resident (title)").[[63]](#cite_note-The_Borneo_Post-63) The Brooke family generally practised a paternalistic form of government with minimal bureaucracy, but were pressured to establish some form of legal framework. Since they were unfamiliar with local customs, the Brooke government created an advisory Supreme Council, mostly consisting of Malay chiefs. This council is the oldest state legislative assembly in Malaysia, with the first general council meeting taking place at [Bintulu](/wiki/Bintulu "Bintulu") in 1867.[[65]](#cite_note-65) In 1928, a judicial commissioner, Thomas Stirling Boyd, was appointed as the first legally trained judge. A similar system relating to matters concerning various Chinese communities was also formed.[[66]](#cite_note-Empirenationalism-66) Members of the local community were encouraged by the Brooke regime to focus on particular functions within the territory; the Ibans and other Dayak people were hired as [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia") while Malays were primarily administrators. Chinese, both local and immigrant, were mostly employed in plantations,[[67]](#cite_note-67) mines and as bureaucrats.[[66]](#cite_note-Empirenationalism-66) Expanding trade led to the formation of the [Borneo Company Limited](/wiki/Borneo_Company_Limited "Borneo Company Limited") in 1856. The company was involved in a wide range of businesses in Sarawak including trade, banking, agriculture, mineral exploration, and development.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Sarawak_territorial_expansion.gif/250px-Sarawak_territorial_expansion.gif)](/wiki/File:Sarawak_territorial_expansion.gif)

Territorial expansion of the [Raj of Sarawak](/wiki/Raj_of_Sarawak "Raj of Sarawak") from 1841 to 1905 played a significant role in defining the current borders of Sarawak.

Between 1853 and 1862, there were a number of uprisings against the Brooke government but all were successfully contained with the aid of local tribes.[[66]](#cite_note-Empirenationalism-66) To guard against future uprisings, a series of forts were constructed to protect Kuching, including [Fort Margherita](/wiki/Fort_Margherita "Fort Margherita"), completed in 1871. By that time Brooke's control of Sarawak was such that defences were largely unnecessary.[[69]](#cite_note-Kingdominjungle-69)

[Charles Anthoni Brooke](/wiki/Charles_Brooke,_Rajah_of_Sarawak "Charles Brooke, Rajah of Sarawak") succeeded his uncle as White Rajah in 1868. Under his rule, Sarawak gained Limbang and the Baram and Trusan valleys from the Sultan of Brunei. The Raj became a British [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") in 1888 with the imperial power responsible for foreign policy and the Brooke government maintaining its local administration.[[70]](#cite_note-Rough_Guides-70) Domestically, Brooke established the [Sarawak Museum](/wiki/Sarawak_Museum "Sarawak Museum") – the oldest museum in Borneo – in 1891,[[69]](#cite_note-Kingdominjungle-69)[[71]](#cite_note-71) and brokered a peace in [Marudi](/wiki/Marudi,_Sarawak "Marudi, Sarawak") by ending intertribal wars there. Economic development continued, with oil wells being drilled from 1910 and the Brooke Dockyard opening two years later. [Anthony Brooke](/wiki/Anthony_Brooke "Anthony Brooke") would become Rajah Muda (heir apparent) in 1939.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

A centenary celebration of Brooke rule in Sarawak was held in 1941. During the celebration, a [new constitution](/wiki/1941_constitution_of_Sarawak "1941 constitution of Sarawak") was introduced that would limit the power of the rajah and grant the Sarawak people a greater role in running the government. However, this constitution was never fully implemented due to the occupation of Sarawak by Japan.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[66]](#cite_note-Empirenationalism-66)[[note 1]](#cite_note-74) That same year saw the United Kingdom withdraw its defensive maritime and air units to Singapore. After the departure of most of the military defenses from Sarawak, the Brooke regime adopted a [scorched earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") policy: The oil production facilities in Miri and the Kuching airfield were to be destroyed, after the airfield had been held for as long as possible. A Japanese invasion force led by [Kiyotake Kawaguchi](/wiki/Kiyotake_Kawaguchi "Kiyotake Kawaguchi") landed in Miri on 16 December 1941 and captured Kuching on 24 December 1941, with British ground forces retreating to [Singkawang](/wiki/Singkawang "Singkawang") in neighbouring [Dutch Borneo](/wiki/Dutch_Borneo "Dutch Borneo"). There, after ten weeks of fighting, the [Allied forces](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") surrendered on 1 April 1942.[[74]](#cite_note-75) [Charles Vyner Brooke](/wiki/Charles_Vyner_Brooke "Charles Vyner Brooke"), the last Rajah of Sarawak, had already left for [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia; his officers were captured by the Japanese and interned at the [Batu Lintang camp](/wiki/Batu_Lintang_camp "Batu Lintang camp").[[75]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Native_population_of_Kuching.JPG/250px-Native_population_of_Kuching.JPG)](/wiki/File:Native_population_of_Kuching.JPG)

Crowds throng a street in [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching") to witness the arrival of [Australian Imperial Force](/wiki/Second_Australian_Imperial_Force "Second Australian Imperial Force") on 12 September 1945.

Sarawak [remained part](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_British_Borneo "Japanese occupation of British Borneo") of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") for three years and eight months. During this time it was divided into three provinces – Kuching-shu, Sibu-shu, and Miri-shu – each under their respective Provincial Governor. The Japanese otherwise preserved the Brooke administrative structure and appointed Japanese people to important government positions.[[76]](#cite_note-77) Allied forces later carried out [Operation Semut](/wiki/Operation_Semut "Operation Semut") to sabotage Japanese operations in Sarawak.[[77]](#cite_note-78) During the [Battle of North Borneo](/wiki/Battle_of_North_Borneo "Battle of North Borneo"), the Australian forces landed at Lutong-Miri area on 20 June 1945 and penetrated as far as [Marudi](/wiki/Marudi,_Sarawak "Marudi, Sarawak") and [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang "Limbang") before halting their operations in Sarawak.[[78]](#cite_note-79) After the [surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan"), the Japanese surrendered to the Australian forces at [Labuan](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan") on 10 September 1945.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81) The following day, the Japanese forces at Kuching surrendered, and the Batu Lintang camp was liberated.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Sarawak was immediately placed under [British Military Administration](/wiki/British_Military_Administration_(Borneo) "British Military Administration (Borneo)") and managed by the [Australian Imperial Force](/wiki/Second_Australian_Imperial_Force "Second Australian Imperial Force") until April 1946.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Lacking the resources to rebuild Sarawak after the war, Charles Vyner Brooke decided to cede Sarawak as British Crown Colony[[84]](#cite_note-85) and a cession bill was put forth in the Council Negri (now [Sarawak State Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Sarawak_State_Legislative_Assembly "Sarawak State Legislative Assembly")), which was debated for three days. The bill passed on 17 May 1946 with 19 votes to 16.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Hundreds of Malay civil servants resigned in protest, sparking an [anti-cession movement](/wiki/Anti-cession_movement_of_Sarawak "Anti-cession movement of Sarawak") and the assassination of the second colonial governor of Sarawak, Sir [Duncan Stewart](/wiki/Duncan_Stewart_(colonial_administrator) "Duncan Stewart (colonial administrator)").[[86]](#cite_note-87) Despite the resistance, Sarawak became a [British Crown colony](/wiki/Sarawak_(British_Crown_colony) "Sarawak (British Crown colony)") on 1 July 1946.[[3]](#cite_note-Porritt1997-3) Anthony Brooke opposed the cession of Sarawak to the British Crown,[[87]](#cite_note-88) for which he was banished from Sarawak by the colonial government.[[66]](#cite_note-Empirenationalism-66)[[note 2]](#cite_note-89) He was only allowed to return 17 years later after Sarawak had become part of Malaysia.[[88]](#cite_note-Anthonylegal-90) In 1950 all anti-cession movements in Sarawak ceased after a clamp-down by the colonial government.[[58]](#cite_note-Fairlandsarawak-58)

[![Ningkan holding papers containing the declaration, standing behind a microphone and in front of several guards on a podium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Sarawak_during_the_formation_of_Malaysia_%2816_September_1963%29.jpg/250px-Sarawak_during_the_formation_of_Malaysia_%2816_September_1963%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarawak_during_the_formation_of_Malaysia_(16_September_1963).jpg)

[Tan Sri Datuk Amar](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles "Malay styles and titles") [Stephen Kalong Ningkan](/wiki/Stephen_Kalong_Ningkan "Stephen Kalong Ningkan") declaring the formation of the Federation of Malaysia on 16 September 1963

On 27 May 1961, [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman "Tunku Abdul Rahman"), the prime minister of the [Federation of Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya"), announced a plan to form a greater federation together with [Singapore](/wiki/Colony_of_Singapore "Colony of Singapore"), [Sarawak](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak"), [North Borneo](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_North_Borneo "Crown Colony of North Borneo") and [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), to be called Malaysia. On 17 January 1962, the [Cobbold Commission](/wiki/Cobbold_Commission "Cobbold Commission") was formed to gauge the support of Sarawak and Sabah for the plan; the Commission reported 80 per cent support for federation.[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92) On 23 October 1962, five political parties in Sarawak formed a united front that supported the formation of Malaysia.[[91]](#cite_note-93) Sarawak was [officially granted self-government](/wiki/Sarawak_Self-government_Day "Sarawak Self-government Day") on 22 July 1963,[[3]](#cite_note-Porritt1997-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Mathews2014-4) and federated with Malaya, North Borneo (now [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah")), and Singapore to form the Federation of Malaysia on 16 September 1963.[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95) The governments of the Philippines and Indonesia opposed the new federation, as did the [Brunei People's Party](/wiki/Brunei_People%27s_Party "Brunei People's Party") and Sarawak-based communist groups, and in 1962, the [Brunei Revolt](/wiki/Brunei_Revolt "Brunei Revolt") broke out.[[94]](#cite_note-96) Indonesian president [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") responded by deploying armed volunteers and, later, military forces into Sarawak.[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98) Thousands of Sarawak communist members went to [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan"), Indonesian Borneo, and underwent training with the [Communist Party of Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia"). The most significant engagement of the confrontation was fought at [Plaman Mapu](/wiki/Battle_of_Plaman_Mapu "Battle of Plaman Mapu") in April 1965. The defeat at Plaman Mapu ultimately resulted in the fall of Sukarno and his replacement with [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto") as President of Indonesia.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Negotiations were restarted between Malaysia and Indonesia and led to the end of the confrontation on 11 August 1966.[[98]](#cite_note-Ohiopress-100)[[note 3]](#cite_note-101) Following continued disagreements with the federal government over proportions of taxes to be paid by Singapore to the federation government, Singapore separated from Malaysia on 9 August 1965 and became an independent and sovereign nation while both Sarawak and Sabah remain in the federation.[[99]](#cite_note-102)[[100]](#cite_note-103)

A number of communist groups existed in Sarawak, the first of which, the Sarawak Overseas Chinese Democratic Youth League, formed in 1951.[[58]](#cite_note-Fairlandsarawak-58)[[note 4]](#cite_note-104) Another group, the North Kalimantan Communist Party (NKCP, also known as Clandestine Communist Organisation (CCO) by government sources) was formally set up in 1970.[[101]](#cite_note-105) Weng Min Chyuan and [Bong Kee Chok](/wiki/Bong_Kee_Chok "Bong Kee Chok") were two of the more notable communist leaders involved in the insurgency. As the political scene changed, it grew progressively more difficult for the communists to operate. This led to Bong opening talks with chief minister [Abdul Rahman Ya'kub](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Ya%27kub "Abdul Rahman Ya'kub") in 1973 and eventually signing an agreement with the government. Weng, who had moved to China in the mid-1960s but nonetheless retained control of the CCO, pushed for a continued armed insurrection against the government in spite of this agreement. The conflict continued mostly in the [Rajang Delta](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River") region but eventually ended when, on 17 October 1990, the NKCP signed a peace agreement with the Government of Sarawak.[[102]](#cite_note-106)[[103]](#cite_note-chanwong-107)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Politics")]

### Government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Timeline_of_evolution_of_political_parties_in_Sarawak.svg/500px-Timeline_of_evolution_of_political_parties_in_Sarawak.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Timeline_of_evolution_of_political_parties_in_Sarawak.svg)

Timeline of political parties in Sarawak

The head of the Sarawak state is the [Yang di-Pertua Negeri](/wiki/List_of_Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri_of_Sarawak "List of Yang di-Pertua Negeri of Sarawak") (also known as TYT or Governor), a largely symbolic position appointed by the [Yang di-Pertuan Agong](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertuan_Agong "Yang di-Pertuan Agong") (King of Malaysia) on the advice of the Malaysian federal government.[[105]](#cite_note-Governance-109) The position has been held by [Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar](/wiki/Wan_Junaidi_Tuanku_Jaafar "Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar") since 2024.[[106]](#cite_note-110) The TYT appoints the [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Sarawak "Premier of Sarawak"), currently held by [Abang Johari Openg](/wiki/Abang_Johari_Openg "Abang Johari Openg") ([GPS](/wiki/Gabungan_Parti_Sarawak "Gabungan Parti Sarawak")),[[107]](#cite_note-111) as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). Generally, the leader of the party that commands the majority of the state Legislative Assembly is appointed as the chief minister; democratically elected representatives are known as state assemblymen. The state assembly passes laws on subjects that are not under the jurisdiction of the [Parliament of Malaysia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia") such as land administration, employment, forests, immigration, merchant shipping and fisheries. The state government is constituted by the premier, the cabinet ministers and their deputy ministers.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Malaysian_and_Sarawak_passport_entry_stamps.jpg/250px-Malaysian_and_Sarawak_passport_entry_stamps.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysian_and_Sarawak_passport_entry_stamps.jpg)

Because the government of Sarawak controls immigration, foreign nationals entering Sarawak receive an additional entry stamp

To protect the interests of the Sarawakians in the Malaysian federation, special safeguards have been included in the [Constitution of Malaysia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia "Constitution of Malaysia"). These include: control over immigration in and out of the state as well as the residence status of non-Sarawakians and Sabahans, limitations on the practice of law to resident lawyers, independence of the Sarawak High Court from the High Court Peninsular Malaysia, a requirement that the Sarawak Chief Minister be consulted prior to the appointment of the chief judge of the Sarawak High Court, the existence of Native Courts in Sarawak and the power to levy sales tax. Natives in Sarawak enjoy special privileges such as quotas and employment in public service, scholarships, university placements, and business permits.[[109]](#cite_note-113) Local governments in Sarawak are exempt from local council laws enacted by the Malaysian parliament.[[110]](#cite_note-95D-114) This level of autonomy means Sarawak is sometimes referred to as a "region", to differentiate it from less autonomous states.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Kuching_Sarawak_Dewan-Undangan-Negeri-Sarawak-01.jpg/250px-Kuching_Sarawak_Dewan-Undangan-Negeri-Sarawak-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kuching_Sarawak_Dewan-Undangan-Negeri-Sarawak-01.jpg)

The [State Assembly building](/wiki/New_Sarawak_State_Legislative_Assembly_Building "New Sarawak State Legislative Assembly Building") is located near the Kuching waterfront.

Major political parties in Sarawak can be divided into three categories: native Sarawak Bumiputera (PBB and PBM), native Sarawak Dayak (PRS, PDP, PBDSB, etc.), and non-Bumiputera (SUPP, PSB, PBK, etc.); Parties, however, may also include members from more than one group.[[112]](#cite_note-116) The first political party, the Sarawak United Peoples' Party (SUPP), was established in 1959, followed by the Parti Negara Sarawak (PANAS) in 1960 and the Sarawak National Party (SNAP) in 1961. Other major political parties such as Parti Pesaka Sarawak (PESAKA) appeared by 1962.[[58]](#cite_note-Fairlandsarawak-58)[[note 5]](#cite_note-117) These parties later joined the national coalition of the Alliance Party. The Alliance Party (later regrouped into [Barisan Nasional](/wiki/Barisan_Nasional "Barisan Nasional")) has ruled Sarawak since the formation of Malaysia. The opposition in Sarawak has consistently alleged that the ruling coalition uses various types of vote-buying tactics in order to win elections.[[113]](#cite_note-Faisal-118)[[note 6]](#cite_note-119)[[114]](#cite_note-120) [Stephen Kalong Ningkan](/wiki/Stephen_Kalong_Ningkan "Stephen Kalong Ningkan") was the first [Chief Minister of Sarawak](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Sarawak "Chief Minister of Sarawak") from 1963 to 1966 following his landslide victory in local council elections. However, he was ousted in 1966 by [Tawi Sli](/wiki/Tawi_Sli "Tawi Sli") with the help of the [Malaysian federal government](/wiki/Malaysian_government "Malaysian government"), causing the [1966 Sarawak constitutional crisis](/wiki/1966_Sarawak_constitutional_crisis "1966 Sarawak constitutional crisis").[[58]](#cite_note-Fairlandsarawak-58)

[![Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Wan_Junaidi_Tuanku_Jaafar_2023.jpg/250px-Wan_Junaidi_Tuanku_Jaafar_2023.jpg)](/wiki/Wan_Junaidi_Tuanku_Jaafar "Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar")

[Yang di-Pertua Negeri](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri_of_Sarawak "Yang di-Pertua Negeri of Sarawak"), [Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar](/wiki/Wan_Junaidi_Tuanku_Jaafar "Wan Junaidi Tuanku Jaafar")

[![Abang Abdul Rahman Zohari Abang Openg](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Abang_Johari_UNIMAS_meeting.jpg/250px-Abang_Johari_UNIMAS_meeting.jpg)](/wiki/Abang_Abdul_Rahman_Zohari_Abang_Openg "Abang Abdul Rahman Zohari Abang Openg")

[Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Sarawak "Premier of Sarawak"), [Abang Abdul Rahman Zohari Abang Openg](/wiki/Abang_Abdul_Rahman_Zohari_Abang_Openg "Abang Abdul Rahman Zohari Abang Openg")

In 1969, the [first Sarawak state election](/wiki/Sarawak_state_election,_1969 "Sarawak state election, 1969") was held, with members of the Council Negri being directly elected by the voters. This election marked the beginning of ethnic [Melanau](/wiki/Melanau "Melanau") domination in Sarawak politics by [Abdul Rahman Ya'kub](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Ya%27kub "Abdul Rahman Ya'kub") and Abdul Taib Mahmud. In the same year, the North Kalimantan Communist Party (NKCP) which subsequently waged a guerrilla war against the newly elected Sarawak state government, was formed. The party was dissolved after the signing of a peace agreement in 1990.[[103]](#cite_note-chanwong-107) 1973 saw the birth of [Parti Pesaka Bumiputera Bersatu](/wiki/Parti_Pesaka_Bumiputera_Bersatu "Parti Pesaka Bumiputera Bersatu") (PBB) following a merger of several parties.[[115]](#cite_note-DAPJOURNAL-121) This party would later become the backbone of the Sarawak BN coalition. In 1978, the [Democratic Action Party](/wiki/Democratic_Action_Party "Democratic Action Party") (DAP) was the first West Malaysia-based party to open its branches in Sarawak.[[115]](#cite_note-DAPJOURNAL-121) Sarawak originally held state elections together with national parliamentary elections. However, the then chief minister Abdul Rahman Ya'kub delayed the dissolution of the state assembly by a year to prepare for the challenges posed by opposition parties.[[113]](#cite_note-Faisal-118) This made Sarawak the only state in Malaysia to hold state elections separate from the national parliamentary elections since 1979.[[116]](#cite_note-122) In 1983, SNAP started to fragment into several splinter parties due to recurrent leadership crises.[[117]](#cite_note-123)[[118]](#cite_note-124) The political climate in the state was stable until the [1987 Ming Court Affair](/wiki/1987_Ming_Court_Affair "1987 Ming Court Affair"), a political coup initiated by [Abdul Taib Mahmud](/wiki/Abdul_Taib_Mahmud "Abdul Taib Mahmud")'s uncle to topple the Taib-led BN coalition. However, the coup was unsuccessful and Taib retained his position as chief minister.[[119]](#cite_note-125)

Since the [2006 state election](/wiki/Sarawak_state_election,_2006 "Sarawak state election, 2006"), the Democratic Action Party (DAP) has derived the majority of its support from urban centres and became the largest opposition party in Sarawak.[[120]](#cite_note-126) In 2010, it formed the [Pakatan Rakyat](/wiki/Pakatan_Rakyat "Pakatan Rakyat") coalition with [Parti Keadilan Rakyat](/wiki/Parti_Keadilan_Rakyat "Parti Keadilan Rakyat") (PKR) and [Parti Islam Se-Malaysia](/wiki/Parti_Islam_Se-Malaysia "Parti Islam Se-Malaysia") (PAS); the latter two parties had become active in Sarawak between 1996 and 2001.[[121]](#cite_note-127) Sarawak is the only state in Malaysia where West Malaysia-based component parties in the BN coalition, especially the [United Malays National Organisation](/wiki/United_Malays_National_Organisation "United Malays National Organisation") (UMNO), have not been active in state politics.[[122]](#cite_note-128)

On 12 June 2018, the [Sarawak Parties Alliance](/wiki/Gabungan_Parti_Sarawak "Gabungan Parti Sarawak") was formed by the BN parties in the state in the aftermath of an historic meeting of party leaders in Kuching, where they decided that in light of the BN defeat in the [2018 Malaysian general election](/wiki/Malaysian_general_election,_2018 "Malaysian general election, 2018") and the changing national situation and a new government, the parties will leave the BN altogether.[[123]](#cite_note-129) In conjunction with the celebration of [Malaysia Day](/wiki/Malaysia_Day "Malaysia Day") in 2018 under the new government, Prime Minister [Mahathir Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad") has promised to restore the status of Sarawak (together with Sabah) as an equal partner to Malaya, where all three parties (and then, Singapore) formed Malaysia in accordance to the [Malaysia Agreement](/wiki/Malaysia_Agreement "Malaysia Agreement").[[124]](#cite_note-130)[[125]](#cite_note-131) However, through the process of the proposed [amendment to the Constitution of Malaysia](/wiki/Proposed_2019_amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Malaysia "Proposed 2019 amendment to the Constitution of Malaysia") in 2019, the bill for the amendment failed to pass following the failure to reach two-thirds majority support (148 votes) in the Parliament with only 138 agreed with the move while 59 abstained from the voting.[[126]](#cite_note-132)[[127]](#cite_note-133) On 14 December 2021, the proposed amendment was passed in the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia") unanimously with 199 votes in favour, and 21 MPs absent from the 6-hour long debate.[[128]](#cite_note-134)

On 26 November 2015, it was announced that the [Kuching Division](/wiki/Kuching_Division "Kuching Division") district of Serian would become Sarawak's 12th division and it had officiated by [Adenan Satem](/wiki/Adenan_Satem "Adenan Satem") at its formal creation on 11 April 2015.[[132]](#cite_note-dv-138)

A division is divided into districts, each headed by a district officer, which are in turn divided into sub-districts, each headed by a Sarawak Administrative Officer (SAO). There is also one development officer for each division and district to implement development projects. The state government appoints a headman (known as *ketua kampung* or *penghulu*) for each village.[[105]](#cite_note-Governance-109)[[129]](#cite_note-Divisiondistrict-135) There are a total of 26 sub-districts in Sarawak all under the jurisdiction of the Sarawak Ministry of Local Government and Community Development.[[133]](#cite_note-139) The list of divisions, districts, sub-districts and their local authorities is shown in the table below:[[134]](#cite_note-State_Planning_Unit_stats-140)

## Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Security")]

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Military")]

The first paramilitary armed forces in Sarawak, a regiment formed by the Brooke regime in 1862, were known as the [Sarawak Rangers](/wiki/Sarawak_Rangers "Sarawak Rangers").[[135]](#cite_note-141) The regiment, renowned for its jungle tracking skills, served in the campaign to end the intertribal wars in Sarawak. It also engaged in guerrilla warfare against the Japanese, in the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency") (in West Malaysia) and the [Sarawak Communist Insurgency](/wiki/Sarawak_Communist_Insurgency "Sarawak Communist Insurgency") against the communists. Following the formation of Malaysia, the regiment was absorbed into the Malaysian military forces and is now known as the [Royal Ranger Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Ranger_Regiment "Royal Ranger Regiment").[[136]](#cite_note-142)

In 1888, Sarawak, together with neighbouring [North Borneo](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo"), and Brunei, became [British protectorates](/wiki/British_protectorates "British protectorates"), and the responsibility for foreign policy was handed over to the British in exchange for military protection.[[70]](#cite_note-Rough_Guides-70) Since the formation of Malaysia, the [Malaysian federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia") has been solely responsible for foreign policy and military forces in the country.[[137]](#cite_note-143)[[138]](#cite_note-144)

### Territorial disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Territorial disputes")]

See also: [Territorial disputes in the South China Sea](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_in_the_South_China_Sea "Territorial disputes in the South China Sea")

Several border disputes between Malaysia and its neighbouring countries concern Sarawak. Land and maritime disputes exist with Brunei.[[139]](#cite_note-145) In 2009, Malaysian prime minister [Abdullah Ahmad Badawi](/wiki/Abdullah_Ahmad_Badawi "Abdullah Ahmad Badawi") claimed that in a meeting with Sultan of Brunei, Brunei agreed to drop its claim over [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang_District "Limbang District").[[140]](#cite_note-146) This was however denied by the second Foreign Minister of Brunei [Lim Jock Seng](/wiki/Lim_Jock_Seng "Lim Jock Seng"), stating the issue was never discussed during the meeting.[[141]](#cite_note-147) [James Shoal](/wiki/James_Shoal "James Shoal") (Betting Serupai) and the [Luconia Shoals](/wiki/Luconia_Shoals "Luconia Shoals") (Betting Raja Jarum/Patinggi Ali), [islands in](/wiki/South_China_Sea_islands "South China Sea islands") the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), fall within Sarawak's [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone"), but concerns have been raised about Chinese incursions.[[142]](#cite_note-148)[[143]](#cite_note-149)[[144]](#cite_note-150) There are also several Sarawak–Kalimantan border issues with Indonesia.[[145]](#cite_note-151)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Julan_waterfall_01.jpg/250px-Julan_waterfall_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Julan_waterfall_01.jpg)

Julan waterfall (located at [Usun Apau Plieran](/wiki/Usun_Apau_Plieran "Usun Apau Plieran")) is the highest waterfall in Sarawak[[146]](#cite_note-152)

The total land area of Sarawak is nearly 124,450 km2 (48,050 sq mi),[[7]](#cite_note-statistics-7) making up 37.5 per cent of the total area of Malaysia, and lies between the northern latitudes 0° 50′ and 5° and eastern longitudes 109° 36′ and 115° 40′ E.[[147]](#cite_note-stateplanning-153) Its 750 km (470 mi) of coastline is interrupted in the north by about 150 km (93 mi) of Bruneian coast.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154) A total of its 45.5 km (28.3 mi) coastline have been [eroding](/wiki/Coastal_erosion "Coastal erosion").[[149]](#cite_note-155) In 1961, Sarawak including neighbouring [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"), which had been included in the [International Maritime Organization](/wiki/International_Maritime_Organization "International Maritime Organization") (IMO) through the participation of the United Kingdom, became joint associate members of the IMO.[[150]](#cite_note-156) Sarawak is separated from Kalimantan Borneo by ranges of high hills and mountains that are part of the central mountain range of Borneo. These become loftier to the north, and are highest near the source of the Baram River at the steep [Mount Batu Lawi](/wiki/Bukit_Batu_Lawi "Bukit Batu Lawi") and [Mount Mulu](/wiki/Mount_Mulu "Mount Mulu"). [Mount Murud](/wiki/Mount_Murud "Mount Murud") is the highest point in Sarawak.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)

Sarawak has a [tropical geography](/wiki/Tropical_geography "Tropical geography") with an [equatorial climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate") and experiences two [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") seasons: a northeast monsoon and a southwest monsoon. The northeast monsoon occurs between November and February, bringing heavy rainfall while the southwest monsoon, which occurs between March and October, brings somewhat less rainfall. The climate is stable throughout the year except for the two monsoons, with average daily temperature varying between 23 °C (73 °F) in the morning to 32 °C (90 °F) in the afternoon at coastal areas. [Miri](/wiki/Miri,_Malaysia "Miri, Malaysia") has the lowest average temperatures in comparison to other major towns in Sarawak and has the longest daylight hours (more than six hours a day), while other areas receive sunshine for five to six hours a day. Humidity is usually high, exceeding 68 per cent, with annual rainfall varying between 330 cm (130 in) and 460 cm (180 in) for up to 220 days a year.[[147]](#cite_note-stateplanning-153) At highland areas, the temperature can vary from 16 °C (61 °F) to 25 °C (77 °F) during the day and as low as 11 °C (52 °F) during the night.[[151]](#cite_note-157)

Sarawak is divided into three [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"). The coastal region is rather low-lying and flat with large areas of swamp and other wet environments. Beaches in Sarawak include Pasir Panjang[[152]](#cite_note-158) and Damai beaches in Kuching,[[153]](#cite_note-159) Tanjung Batu beach in Bintulu,[[154]](#cite_note-160) and Tanjung Lobang[[155]](#cite_note-161) and Hawaii beaches in Miri.[[156]](#cite_note-162) Hilly terrain accounts for much of the inhabited land and is where most of the cities and towns are found. The [ports](/wiki/Port "Port") of Kuching and [Sibu](/wiki/Sibu "Sibu") are built some distance from the coast on rivers while Bintulu and Miri are close to the coastline where the hills stretch right to the South China Sea. The third region is the mountainous region along the Sarawak–Kalimantan border, where a number of villages such as [Bario](/wiki/Bario "Bario"), [Ba'kelalan](/wiki/Ba%27kelalan "Ba'kelalan"), and [Usun Apau Plieran](/wiki/Usun_Apau_Plieran "Usun Apau Plieran") are located.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154) A number of rivers flow through Sarawak, with the [Sarawak River](/wiki/Sarawak_River "Sarawak River") being the main river flowing through Kuching. The [Rajang River](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River") is the longest river in Malaysia, measuring 563 km (350 mi) including its tributary, [Balleh River](/wiki/Balleh_River "Balleh River"). To the north, the Baram, Limbang and Trusan Rivers drain into the [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay").[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Rajang_delta.jpg/250px-Rajang_delta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rajang_delta.jpg)

The [Rajang River](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River") is the longest river in Malaysia

Sarawak can be divided into two geological zones: the [Sunda Shield](/wiki/Sunda_Plate "Sunda Plate"), which extends southwest from the Batang Lupar River (near [Sri Aman](/wiki/Simanggang "Simanggang")) and forms the southern tip of Sarawak, and the [geosyncline](/wiki/Geosyncline "Geosyncline") region, which extends northeast to the Batang Lupar River, forming the central and northern regions of Sarawak. The oldest rock type in southern Sarawak is [schist](/wiki/Schist "Schist") formed during the [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") and [Lower Permian](/wiki/Permian "Permian") times, while the youngest [igneous rock](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock") in this region, [andesite](/wiki/Andesite "Andesite"), can be found at [Sematan](/wiki/Sematan "Sematan"). Geological formation of the central and northern regions started during the late [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") period. Other types of stone that can be found in central and northern Sarawak are [shale](/wiki/Shale "Shale"), [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), and [chert](/wiki/Chert "Chert").[[147]](#cite_note-stateplanning-153) The Miri Division in eastern Sarawak is the region of [Neogene](/wiki/Neogene "Neogene") strata containing organic rich rock formations which are the prolific [oil and gas reserves](/wiki/Oil_reserves "Oil reserves"). The rocks enriched in organic components are [mudstones](/wiki/Mudstone "Mudstone") in Lambir, Miri and Tukau Formations of [Middle Miocene](/wiki/Middle_Miocene "Middle Miocene")-[Lower Pliocene](/wiki/Lower_Pliocene "Lower Pliocene") age.[[157]](#cite_note-163) Significant quantities of Sarawak soil are [lithosols](/wiki/Orthent "Orthent"), up to 60 per cent, and [podsols](/wiki/Podzol "Podzol"), around 12 per cent, while abundant [alluvial soil](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium") is found in coastal and riverine regions. 12 per cent of Sarawak is covered with [peat swamp forest](/wiki/Peat_swamp_forest "Peat swamp forest").[[147]](#cite_note-stateplanning-153) [Limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") with well-developed [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") topography and cave systems is found scattered from west to east Sarawak, but concentrated in certain regions such as in the Bau district in the west and southwards near the Kalimantan border.[[158]](#cite_note-164)

There are thirty national parks,[[159]](#cite_note-165) among which are [Niah](/wiki/Niah_National_Park "Niah National Park") with its eponymous caves,[[160]](#cite_note-166) the highly developed ecosystem around [Lambir Hills](/wiki/Lambir_Hills_National_Park "Lambir Hills National Park"),[[161]](#cite_note-167) and the [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") of [Gunung Mulu](/wiki/Gunung_Mulu_National_Park "Gunung Mulu National Park").[[162]](#cite_note-168)[[163]](#cite_note-169) The last contains [Sarawak Chamber](/wiki/Sarawak_Chamber "Sarawak Chamber"), one of the world's largest underground chambers,[[164]](#cite_note-170) [Deer Cave](/wiki/Deer_Cave "Deer Cave"), the largest cave passage in the world,[[165]](#cite_note-171) and [Clearwater Cave](/wiki/Clearwater_Cave "Clearwater Cave"), the longest cave system in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[166]](#cite_note-172)[[167]](#cite_note-173)

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

See also: [Fauna of Borneo](/wiki/Fauna_of_Borneo "Fauna of Borneo"), [Deforestation in Borneo](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Borneo "Deforestation in Borneo"), and [Sarawak Biodiversity Centre](/wiki/Sarawak_Biodiversity_Centre "Sarawak Biodiversity Centre")

Sarawak contains large tracts of [tropical rainforest](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest") with diverse plant species,[[168]](#cite_note-174) which has led to a number of them being studied for medicinal properties.[[169]](#cite_note-175) [Mangrove](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") and [nipah](/wiki/Nypa_fruticans "Nypa fruticans") forests lining its [estuaries](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") comprise 2% of its forested area, peat swamp forests along other parts of its coastline cover 16%, [Kerangas forest](/wiki/Kerangas_forest "Kerangas forest") covers 5% and [Dipterocarpaceae](/wiki/Dipterocarpaceae "Dipterocarpaceae") forests cover most mountainous areas. The major trees found in estuary forests include *bako* and *nibong*, while those in the peat swamp forests include *ramin* (*[Gonystylus bancanus](/wiki/Gonystylus_bancanus "Gonystylus bancanus")*), *meranti* (*[Shorea](/wiki/Shorea "Shorea")*), and *medang jongkong* (*[Dactylocladus stenostachys](/wiki/Dactylocladus_stenostachys "Dactylocladus stenostachys")*).[[147]](#cite_note-stateplanning-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/How_to_peel_a_banana_with_your_foot_%2826443349170%29.jpg/250px-How_to_peel_a_banana_with_your_foot_%2826443349170%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:How_to_peel_a_banana_with_your_foot_(26443349170).jpg)

An orangutan peeling a banana at Semenggoh Wildlife Reserve.

Animal species are also highly varied, with 185 species of mammals, 530 species of birds, 166 species of snakes, 104 species of lizards, and 113 species of amphibians, of which 19 per cent of the mammals, 6 per cent of the birds, 20 per cent of the snakes and 32 per cent of the lizards are [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism"). These species are largely found in Totally Protected Areas. There are over 2,000 tree species in Sarawak. Other plants includes 1,000 species of orchids, 757 species of ferns, and 260 species of palm.[[170]](#cite_note-176) The state is the habitat of endangered animals, including the [borneo pygmy elephant](/wiki/Borneo_pygmy_elephant "Borneo pygmy elephant"), [proboscis monkey](/wiki/Proboscis_monkey "Proboscis monkey"), [orangutans](/wiki/Orangutan "Orangutan") and [Sumatran rhinoceroses](/wiki/Sumatran_rhinoceros "Sumatran rhinoceros").[[171]](#cite_note-177) Matang Wildlife Centre, Semenggoh Nature Reserve, and [Lanjak Entimau Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Lanjak_Entimau_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Lanjak Entimau Wildlife Sanctuary")[[172]](#cite_note-178) are noted for their orangutan protection programmes.[[173]](#cite_note-179)[[174]](#cite_note-180) Talang–Satang National Park is notable for its turtle conservation initiatives.[[175]](#cite_note-181) [Birdwatching](/wiki/Birdwatching "Birdwatching") is a common activity in various national parks such as Gunung Mulu National Park, Lambir Hills National Park,[[176]](#cite_note-182) and [Similajau National Park](/wiki/Similajau_National_Park "Similajau National Park").[[177]](#cite_note-183) Miri–Sibuti National Park is known for its coral reefs[[178]](#cite_note-184) and Gunung Gading National Park for its *[Rafflesia](/wiki/Rafflesia "Rafflesia")* flowers.[[179]](#cite_note-185) [Bako National Park](/wiki/Bako_National_Park "Bako National Park"), the oldest national park in Sarawak, is known for its 275 proboscis monkeys,[[180]](#cite_note-186) and Padawan Pitcher Garden for its various carnivorous [pitcher plants](/wiki/Pitcher_plant "Pitcher plant").[[181]](#cite_note-187) In 1854, [Alfred Russel Wallace](/wiki/Alfred_Russel_Wallace "Alfred Russel Wallace") visited Sarawak. A year later, he formulated the "Sarawak Law" which foreshadowed the formulation of his (and [Darwin's](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")) theory of evolution by [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") three years later.[[182]](#cite_note-188)

The Sarawak state government has enacted several laws to protect its forests and endangered wildlife species. Some of the protected species are the orangutan, [green sea turtle](/wiki/Green_sea_turtle "Green sea turtle"), [flying lemur](/wiki/Sunda_flying_lemur "Sunda flying lemur"), and [piping hornbill](/wiki/Piping_hornbill "Piping hornbill"). Under the Wild Life Protection Ordinance 1998, Sarawak natives are given permissions to hunt for a restricted range of wild animals in the jungles but should not possess more than 5 kg (11 lb) of meat.[[183]](#cite_note-189) The Sarawak Forest Department was established in 1919 to conserve forest resources in the state.[[184]](#cite_note-190) Following international criticism of the [logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging") industry in Sarawak, the state government decided to downsize the Sarawak Forest Department and created the Sarawak Forestry Corporation in 1995.[[185]](#cite_note-191)[[186]](#cite_note-192) The Sarawak Biodiversity Centre was set up in 1997 for the conservation, protection, and sustainable development of biodiversity in the state.[[187]](#cite_note-193)

#### Conservation issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Conservation issues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Sarawak%2C_Cultural_Village_11.jpg/250px-Sarawak%2C_Cultural_Village_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarawak,_Cultural_Village_11.jpg)

A logging camp along the [Rajang River](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River")

Sarawak's rain forests are primarily threatened by the logging industry and palm oil plantations.[[188]](#cite_note-194) The issue of human rights of the [Penan](/wiki/Penan "Penan") and deforestation in Sarawak became an international environmental issue when Swiss activist [Bruno Manser](/wiki/Bruno_Manser "Bruno Manser") visited Sarawak regularly between 1984 and 2000.[[189]](#cite_note-195) Deforestation has affected the life of indigenous tribes, especially the Penan, whose livelihood is heavily dependent on forest produce. This led to several blockades by indigenous tribes during the 1980s and 1990s against logging companies encroaching on their lands.[[190]](#cite_note-196) Indeed, illegal logging in particular has decimated the forest regions indigenous populations depend on for their livelihoods, depleting fish, wildlife, but also traditional medicinal herbs and construction staples like Palm.[[191]](#cite_note-197) There have also been cases where [Native Customary Rights](/wiki/Aboriginal_title "Aboriginal title") (NCR) lands have been given to timber and plantation companies without the permission of the locals.[[192]](#cite_note-198) The indigenous people have resorted to legal means to reinstate their NCR. In 2001 the High Court of Sarawak fully reinstated the NCR land claimed by the Rumah Nor people, but this was overturned partially in 2005. However, this case has served as a precedent, leading to more NCR being upheld by the high court in the following years.[[193]](#cite_note-199)[[194]](#cite_note-200) Sarawak's mega-dam policies, such as the [Bakun Dam](/wiki/Bakun_Dam "Bakun Dam") and [Murum Dam](/wiki/Murum_Dam "Murum Dam") projects, have submerged thousands of hectares of forest and displaced thousands of indigenous people.[[195]](#cite_note-201)[[196]](#cite_note-202) Since 2013, the proposed [Baram Dam](/wiki/Baram_Dam "Baram Dam") project has been delayed due to ongoing protests from local indigenous tribes.[[197]](#cite_note-203) Since 2014, the Sarawak government under chief minister [Adenan Satem](/wiki/Adenan_Satem "Adenan Satem") started to take action against [illegal logging](/wiki/Illegal_logging "Illegal logging") in the state and to diversify the economy of the state.[[198]](#cite_note-204) Through the course of 2016 over 2 million acres of forest, much of it in orangutan habitats, were declared protected areas.[[199]](#cite_note-205)

Sources vary as to Sarawak's remaining forest cover: former chief minister [Abdul Taib Mahmud](/wiki/Abdul_Taib_Mahmud "Abdul Taib Mahmud") declared that it fell from 70% to 48% between 2011 and 2012, the Sarawak Forest Department and the Ministry of Resource Planning and Environment both held that it remained at 80% in 2012,[[200]](#cite_note-Taibawangforests-206)[[201]](#cite_note-207) and [Wetlands International](/wiki/Wetlands_International "Wetlands International") reported that it fell by 10% between 2005 and 2010, 3.5 times faster than the rest of Asia combined.[[202]](#cite_note-208)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Sarawak](/wiki/Economy_of_Sarawak "Economy of Sarawak")

Sarawak GDP share by sector (2021)[[203]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy2021-209)

1. Services 38.1 (37.1%)
2. Manufacturing 24.9 (24.2%)
3. Mining & Quarrying 20.6 (20.0%)
4. Agriculture 14 (13.6%)
5. Construction 4.9 (4.77%)
6. Import Duties 0.3 (0.29%)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Bintulu_LNG_port.jpg/250px-Bintulu_LNG_port.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bintulu_LNG_port.jpg)

An LNG port at Bintulu, Sarawak

Historically, Sarawak's economy was stagnant during the rule of previous three white Rajahs. After the formation of Malaysia, Sarawak GDP growth rate has risen due to increase in petroleum output and the rise in global petroleum prices. However, the state economy is less diversified and still heavily dependent upon the export of primary commodities when compared to Malaysia overall. The per capita GDP in Sarawak was lower than the national average from 1970 to 1990.[[204]](#cite_note-210) As of 2021, GDP per capita for Sarawak stands at RM 65,971 (US$15,173),[[9]](#cite_note-Dayak_Daily-9) which is the third highest in Malaysia. However, the urban-rural income gap remained a major problem in Sarawak.[[205]](#cite_note-211)

Sarawak is abundant in natural resources, and primary industries such as mining, agriculture, and forestry accounted for 32.8% of its economy in 2013.[[206]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy-212) It also specialises in the manufacture of food and beverages, wood-based and [rattan](/wiki/Rattan "Rattan") products, basic metal products, and [petrochemicals](/wiki/Petrochemical "Petrochemical"),[[134]](#cite_note-State_Planning_Unit_stats-140) as well as cargo and air services and tourism.[[206]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy-212)
The state's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) grew by 40.5% per year on average from 2010 to 2021,[[207]](#cite_note-213) but became very more volatile later on, ranging from −3.0% in 2009 to 35.0% in 2021. Sarawak contributed 9.5% of Malaysia's GDP in the nine years leading up to 2020, making it the third largest contributor after [Selangor](/wiki/Selangor "Selangor") and [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur").[[206]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy-212) From 2015 to 2021, the oil and gas industry accounted for 55.2% of the Sarawak government's revenue. It attracted RM 80 billion (US$17.85 billion) in foreign investments, with 95% going to the [Sarawak Corridor of Renewable Energy](/wiki/Sarawak_Corridor_of_Renewable_Energy "Sarawak Corridor of Renewable Energy") (SCORE), the second largest [economic corridor](/wiki/Economic_corridor "Economic corridor") in Malaysia.[[206]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy-212)

Sarawak has an estimate of 800 million barrels of [proven oil reserves](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_proven_oil_reserves "List of countries by proven oil reserves") with further are to be discovered based on recent findings.[[208]](#cite_note-214) The export-oriented economy is dominated by [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") (LNG), which accounts for more than half of total exports. Crude petroleum accounts for 45.1%, while palm oil, [sawlogs](/wiki/Sawlog "Sawlog"), and [sawn timber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber") account for 12.0% collectively.[[206]](#cite_note-Sarawakeconomy-212) The state receives a 5% [royalty](/wiki/Royalty_payment "Royalty payment") from [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas "Petronas") over oil explorations in its territorial waters.[[209]](#cite_note-215) Most of the oil and gas deposits are located offshore next to Bintulu and Miri at [Balingian](/wiki/Balingian "Balingian") basin, Baram basin, and around Luconia Shoals.[[210]](#cite_note-216)

Sarawak is one of the world's largest exporters of tropical [hardwood timber](/wiki/Hardwood_timber_production "Hardwood timber production"), constituting 65% of the total Malaysian log exports in 2000. The last [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") statistics in 2001 estimated Sarawak's sawlog exports at an average of 14,109,000 m3 (498,300,000 cu ft) per year between 1996 and 2000.[[211]](#cite_note-217)

In 1955, [OCBC](/wiki/OCBC_Bank "OCBC Bank") became the first foreign bank to operate in Sarawak, with other overseas banks following suit.[[212]](#cite_note-218) Other notable Sarawak-based companies include [Cahya Mata Sarawak Berhad](/wiki/Cahya_Mata_Sarawak_Berhad "Cahya Mata Sarawak Berhad"), Naim Holdings, and [Rimbunan Hijau](/wiki/Rimbunan_Hijau "Rimbunan Hijau").[[213]](#cite_note-219)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Energy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Bakun_Dam_Power_House.jpg/250px-Bakun_Dam_Power_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bakun_Dam_Power_House.jpg)

[Turbines](/wiki/Turbines "Turbines") inside the Bakun Dam power house. The dam is the main source for [electric energy](/wiki/Electric_energy "Electric energy") in Sarawak.

Electricity in Sarawak, supplied by the state-owned [Sarawak Energy](/wiki/Sarawak_Energy "Sarawak Energy") Berhad (SEB),[[214]](#cite_note-Sarawak_Energy_Portfolio-220) is primarily sourced from traditional coal fired power plants and [thermal power stations](/wiki/Thermal_power_station "Thermal power station") using LNG,[[214]](#cite_note-Sarawak_Energy_Portfolio-220)[[215]](#cite_note-221) but diesel based sources and hydroelectricity are also utilised. There are 3 hydroelectric dams as of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit) at [Batang Ai](/wiki/Batang_Ai_Dam "Batang Ai Dam"),[[216]](#cite_note-Sarawaak_HEP_Plan-222) Bakun,[[217]](#cite_note-223) and Murum,[[218]](#cite_note-224) with several others under construction.[[216]](#cite_note-Sarawaak_HEP_Plan-222) In early 2016, SEB signed Malaysia's first energy export deal to supply electricity to neighbouring [West Kalimantan](/wiki/West_Kalimantan "West Kalimantan") in Indonesia.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

In 2008, SCORE was established as a framework to develop the energy sector in the state, specifically the Murum, [Baram](/wiki/Baram_Dam "Baram Dam"), and Baleh Dams as well as potential coal-based power plants,[[220]](#cite_note-226) and 10 high priority industries out to 2030.[[221]](#cite_note-227)[[222]](#cite_note-228) The Regional Corridor Development Authority is the government agency responsible for managing SCORE.[[223]](#cite_note-229) The entire central region of Sarawak is covered under SCORE, including areas such as Samalaju (near Bintulu), Tanjung Manis, and Mukah.[[224]](#cite_note-230) Samalaju will be developed as an industrial park,[[225]](#cite_note-231) with Tanjung Manis as a [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal") food hub,[[226]](#cite_note-232) and Mukah as the administrative centre for SCORE with a focus on resource-based research and development.[[227]](#cite_note-233)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/French_Gypsy_band_performing_during_RWMF_2006.jpg/250px-French_Gypsy_band_performing_during_RWMF_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:French_Gypsy_band_performing_during_RWMF_2006.jpg)

French [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") Manouche band performing during [Rainforest World Music Festival](/wiki/Rainforest_World_Music_Festival "Rainforest World Music Festival") 2006

Tourism plays a major role in the economy of Sarawak and contributed 7.9% of the state's GDP in 2016.[[228]](#cite_note-tourist2016-234)
Foreign visitors to Sarawak are predominantly from Brunei, Indonesia, the Philippines, Singapore, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Germany, Australia, Belgium, South Korea, and United Kingdom.[[229]](#cite_note-tourist2017-235) A number of different organisations, both state and private, are involved in the promotion of tourism in Sarawak: the Sarawak Tourism Board is the state body responsible for tourism promotion in the state, various private tourism groups are united under the Sarawak Tourism Federation, and the Sarawak Convention Bureau is responsible for attracting conventions, conferences, and corporate events which are held in the [Borneo Convention Centre in Kuching](/wiki/Borneo_Convention_Centre_Kuching "Borneo Convention Centre Kuching").[[230]](#cite_note-Oxford_Business_Group_Tourism-236) The public and private bodies in Sarawak hold a biannual event to award the Sarawak Hornbill Tourism Award, an award for achievements within various categories, to recognise businesses and individuals for their efforts in the development of tourism within the state.[[231]](#cite_note-237)

The [Rainforest World Music Festival](/wiki/Rainforest_World_Music_Festival "Rainforest World Music Festival") is the region's primary musical event, attracting more than 50,000 people annually.[[232]](#cite_note-238) Other events that are held regularly in Sarawak are the Anugerah Seri Angkasa (ASA), ASEAN International Film Festival, [Borneo Music Festival Live](/w/index.php?title=Borneo_Music_Festival_Live&action=edit&redlink=1 "Borneo Music Festival Live (page does not exist)"), [Borneo Jazz Festival](/wiki/Borneo_Jazz_Festival "Borneo Jazz Festival"), [Borneo Cultural Festival](/wiki/Borneo_Cultural_Festival "Borneo Cultural Festival"), and Borneo International Kite Festival.[[230]](#cite_note-Oxford_Business_Group_Tourism-236) Major shopping complexes in Sarawak include The Spring, Boulevard, VivaCity, Plaza Merdeka, City One, and AEON shopping malls in Kuching,[[233]](#cite_note-239) Bintang Megamall, Boulevard, Imperial Mall, and Miri Plaza shopping malls in Miri[[234]](#cite_note-240) and NAIM Bintulu Paragon Street Mall, [The Spring](/wiki/The_Spring_Bintulu "The Spring Bintulu"), Boulevard Shopping Mall, Crown Pacific Mall, Commerce Square Mall, Times Square Megamall, and Parkcity Mall in Bintulu.
