## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Infrastructure development in Sarawak is overseen by the Ministry of Infrastructure Development and Transportation, successor to the Ministry of Infrastructure Development and Communications (MIDCom) after it was renamed in 2016.[[239]](#cite_note-245) Despite this ministerial oversight, infrastructure in Sarawak remains relatively underdeveloped compared to [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia").[[240]](#cite_note-246)

In 2009, 94% of urban Sarawak was supplied with electricity, but only 67% of rural areas had electricity.[[241]](#cite_note-247) However, this had increased to 91% by 2014.[[242]](#cite_note-water_and_electricity-248) According to a 2015 article, household internet penetration in Sarawak was lower than Malaysian national average, 41.2% versus 58.6%, with 58.5% of internet use being in urban areas and 29.9% in rural areas. In comparison, mobile telecommunication uptake in Sarawak was comparable to the national average, 93.3% against a national average of 94.2%, and on par with neighbouring Sabah.[[243]](#cite_note-249) Mobile telecommunication infrastructure, specifically broadcast towers, are built and managed by Sacofa Sdn Bhd (Sacofa Private Limited), which enjoys a monopoly in Sarawak after the company was granted a 20-year exclusivity deal on the provision, maintenance and leasing of towers in the state.[[244]](#cite_note-250)

A number of different bodies manage the supply of water depending on their region of responsibility, including the Kuching Water Board (KWB) which manages Kuching water supply, Sibu Water Board (SWB) which manages Sibu water supply, LAKU Management Sdn Bhd, which handle water supply in Miri, Bintulu, and Limbang[[245]](#cite_note-Transport_and_Infrastructure-251) and the Rural Water Supply Department managing the water supply for the remaining areas.[[246]](#cite_note-252) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit), 82% of the rural areas have a fresh water supply.[[242]](#cite_note-water_and_electricity-248)

### Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

Sarawak launched its radio service on 7 June 1954, which became a part of Radio Malaysia when it joined Malaysia in 1963 and later part of the bigger [Radio Televisyen Malaysia](/wiki/Radio_Televisyen_Malaysia "Radio Televisyen Malaysia") (RTM) in 1969 when the nation's radio and television operations merged.[[247]](#cite_note-253) It did not have television service until 30 August 1975, when [RTM TV1](/wiki/TV1_(Malaysian_TV_network) "TV1 (Malaysian TV network)") was made available for East Malaysian viewers.[[248]](#cite_note-254) RTM has six branches in the state - a main office in capital city Kuching and five other offices in Simanggang, Sibu, Bintulu, Miri and Limbang. The main office produces news and shows for RTM's television channels and operates four state radio channels, namely Sarawak FM, Red FM and Wai FM Iban and Bidayuh networks, whereas five other offices operate district radio channels such as Sri Aman FM, Sibu FM, Bintulu FM, Miri FM and Limbang FM. On 7 April 1998, [NTV7](/wiki/NTV7 "NTV7") was launched by Sarawakian businessman [Mohd Effendi Norwawi](/wiki/Mohd_Effendi_Norwawi "Mohd Effendi Norwawi") under the ownership of Natseven TV Sdn Bhd. Before its acquisition by [Media Prima Berhad](/wiki/Media_Prima "Media Prima") in 2005, it had a studio in the state capital. However, Sarawak didn't have its own true TV station until 10 October 2020, when it launched [TVS](/wiki/TVS_(Malaysian_TV_channel) "TVS (Malaysian TV channel)"), thus becoming the first region in Malaysia to own its TV station. It is currently available in [Astro](/wiki/Astro_(television) "Astro (television)"), [Astro NJOI](/wiki/Astro_NJOI "Astro NJOI") and [myFreeview (MYTV Broadcasting)](/wiki/MYTV_Broadcasting "MYTV Broadcasting") and available in 4 languages: Malay, English, Iban and Chinese (Mandarin). Commercial radio channels based in the state include [Cats FM](/wiki/Cats_FM "Cats FM") and [TEA FM](/wiki/TEA_FM "TEA FM"), respectively launched on 8 August 1996 and 1 August 2015.[[249]](#cite_note-255)[[250]](#cite_note-256)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/KIA_newterminal.jpg/250px-KIA_newterminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:KIA_newterminal.jpg)

[Kuching International Airport](/wiki/Kuching_International_Airport "Kuching International Airport") terminal

Much like many former British territories, Sarawak uses a [dual carriageway](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway") with [the left-hand traffic rule](/wiki/Left-_and_right-hand_traffic "Left- and right-hand traffic").[[251]](#cite_note-257) As of 2013, Sarawak had a total of 32,091 km (19,940 mi) of connected roadways, with 18,003 km (11,187 mi) being paved state routes, 8,313 km (5,165 mi) of dirt tracks, 4,352 km (2,704 mi) of gravel roads, and 1,424 km (885 mi) of paved federal highway. The primary route in Sarawak is the [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway"), which runs from Sematan, Sarawak, through Brunei to [Tawau](/wiki/Tawau "Tawau"), Sabah.[[252]](#cite_note-Oxford_transport-258) Despite being a major highway, the condition of the road was poor leading to numerous accidents and fatalities.[[253]](#cite_note-259) 16 billion ringgit worth of contracts were awarded to a number of local companies in December 2016 to add new vehicle and pedestrian bridges, interchanges and bus shelters to the highway as part of a multi-phase project.[[254]](#cite_note-260) However since 2025, the highway is nearing completion with a majority of the stretch being fully usable, cutting down travel time significantly.[[255]](#cite_note-261)

A [railway line](/wiki/Sarawak_Railway_Line "Sarawak Railway Line") existed before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), but the last remnants of the line were dismantled in 1959.[[256]](#cite_note-262) A rail project was announced in 2008 to be in line with the transport needs of SCORE, but as yet no construction work has begun despite an anticipated completion date in 2015.[[257]](#cite_note-263) In 2017, the Sarawak government proposed a [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system connecting Kuching, Samarahan and Serian divisions[[258]](#cite_note-264) which eventually culminated into an [autonomous rail rapid transit](/wiki/Autonomous_Rail_Rapid_Transit "Autonomous Rail Rapid Transit") system ([Kuching Urban Transportation System](/wiki/Kuching_Urban_Transportation_System "Kuching Urban Transportation System")) with anticipated completion in 2028 for Phase 1.[[259]](#cite_note-265) There are also proposals to introduce a similar system to Bintulu in the future.[[260]](#cite_note-266) Currently, buses are the primary mode of public transportation in Sarawak with interstate services connecting the state to Sabah, Brunei, and [Pontianak](/wiki/Pontianak,_Indonesia "Pontianak, Indonesia") (Indonesia).[[245]](#cite_note-Transport_and_Infrastructure-251)

Sarawak is served by a number of airports with [Kuching International Airport](/wiki/Kuching_International_Airport "Kuching International Airport"), located south west of Kuching, being the largest. Flights from Kuching are mainly to [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") but also to [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru"), [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), Sabah, Kelantan, Singapore and Pontianak, Indonesia. A second airport at [Miri](/wiki/Miri_Airport "Miri Airport") serves flights primarily to other Malaysian states as well as services to Singapore. Other smaller airports such as [Sibu Airport](/wiki/Sibu_Airport "Sibu Airport"), [Bintulu Airport](/wiki/Bintulu_Airport "Bintulu Airport"), [Mukah Airport](/wiki/Mukah_Airport "Mukah Airport"), [Marudi Airport](/wiki/Marudi_Airport "Marudi Airport"), [Mulu Airport](/wiki/Mulu_Airport "Mulu Airport"), and [Limbang Airport](/wiki/Limbang_Airport "Limbang Airport") provide domestic services within Malaysia. There are also a number of remote airstrips serving rural communities in the state.[[252]](#cite_note-Oxford_transport-258) Three airlines serve flights in Sarawak, [Malaysia Airlines](/wiki/Malaysia_Airlines "Malaysia Airlines"), [Air Asia](/wiki/Air_Asia "Air Asia"), and [AirBorneo](/wiki/AirBorneo "AirBorneo") all of which use Kuching Airport as their main hub.[[261]](#cite_note-267) The state owned [Hornbill Skyways](/wiki/Hornbill_Skyways "Hornbill Skyways") is an aviation company that largely provides private chartered flights and flight services for public servants.[[262]](#cite_note-268)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/RTG_at_Bintulu_International_Container_Terminal_%28BICT%29.jpg/250px-RTG_at_Bintulu_International_Container_Terminal_%28BICT%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:RTG_at_Bintulu_International_Container_Terminal_(BICT).jpg)

Bintulu International Container Terminal (BICT) at Bintulu seaport

Sarawak has four primary ports located at Kuching, Sibu, Bintulu, and Miri.[[245]](#cite_note-Transport_and_Infrastructure-251) The busiest seaport at Bintulu is under the jurisdiction of the Malaysian federal government and mainly handles LNG products and regular cargo. The remaining ports are under the respective state port authorities. The combined throughput of the four primary ports was 61.04 million freight weight tonnes (FWT) in 2013.[[252]](#cite_note-Oxford_transport-258) Sarawak has 55 navigable river networks with a combined length of 3,300 km (2,100 mi). For centuries, the rivers of Sarawak have been a primary means of transport as well as a route for timber and other agricultural goods moving downriver for export at the country's major ports. Sibu port, located 113 km (70 mi) from the river's mouth, is the main hub along the Rajang River mainly handling timber products. However, the throughput of Sibu port has declined over the years after Tanjung Manis Industrial Port (TIMP) began operating further downriver.[[252]](#cite_note-Oxford_transport-258)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

See also: [List of hospitals in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Malaysia "List of hospitals in Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/27_August_2011_Sarawak_General_Hospital.jpg/250px-27_August_2011_Sarawak_General_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:27_August_2011_Sarawak_General_Hospital.jpg)

The [Sarawak General Hospital](/wiki/Sarawak_General_Hospital "Sarawak General Hospital")

Health care in provided by three major government hospitals, [Sarawak General Hospital](/wiki/Sarawak_General_Hospital "Sarawak General Hospital"), [Sibu Hospital](/wiki/Sibu_Hospital "Sibu Hospital"), and [Miri Hospital](/wiki/Miri_Hospital "Miri Hospital"),[[263]](#cite_note-269) as well as numerous district hospitals,[[264]](#cite_note-Sarawak_specialists-270) public health clinics, community clinics, and rural clinics.[[265]](#cite_note-Oxford_healthcare-271) Sarawak Heart Centre was set up in 2011 in [Kota Samarahan](/wiki/Kota_Samarahan "Kota Samarahan") to provide cardiology services to patients.[[266]](#cite_note-272) Besides government-owned hospitals and clinics, there are several private hospitals in Sarawak[[267]](#cite_note-273) such as the Normah Medical Specialists Centre, Timberland Medical Specialists Centre,[[268]](#cite_note-274) and Sibu Specialist Medical Centre. Hospitals in Sarawak typically provide the full gamut of health care options, from triage to palliative care for the terminally ill. In 1994, Sarawak General Hospital Department of Radiotherapy, Oncology & Palliative Care instituted an at-home care, or [hospice](/wiki/Hospice "Hospice") care, program for cancer patients. The non profit Sarawak Hospice Society was established in 1998 to promote this program.[[269]](#cite_note-275)
In comparison to the number of other medical facilities, mental health is only serviced by a single facility, Hospital Sentosa.[[270]](#cite_note-276) This abundance of medical services has made Sarawak a medical tourism destination for visitors from neighbouring Brunei and Indonesia.[[271]](#cite_note-277)

In comparison to the prevalence of health services in urban regions, much of rural Sarawak is only accessible by river transport, which limits access.[[272]](#cite_note-278) Remote rural areas that are beyond the operating areas of health clinics, about 12 km (7.5 mi),[[273]](#cite_note-279) and inaccessible by land or river are serviced by a monthly flying doctor service, which was established in 1973.[[274]](#cite_note-280)
A village health promoter program, where volunteers are provided with basic medical training, was established in 1981 but difficulty in providing medical supplies to remote villages, as well as a lack of incentive, resulted in a decline of the program.[[275]](#cite_note-281) A variety of traditional medicine practices are still being used by the various communities in Sarawak to supplement modern medical practices but this practice is also declining.[[276]](#cite_note-282) However, since 2004, there has been a resurgence in traditional medicine in Malaysia resulting in the establishment of a traditional medicine division within the Ministry of Health. A 2006 government program to have integrated hospitals led to numerous universities starting programs to teach traditional medicine and major hospitals, including Sarawak General Hospital, providing traditional therapies.[[277]](#cite_note-283)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

Main articles: [List of schools in Sarawak](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Sarawak "List of schools in Sarawak") and [List of universities in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Malaysia "List of universities in Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Chancellory_Universiti_Malaysia_Sarawak.JPG/250px-Chancellory_Universiti_Malaysia_Sarawak.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chancellory_Universiti_Malaysia_Sarawak.JPG)

[Universiti Malaysia Sarawak](/wiki/Universiti_Malaysia_Sarawak "Universiti Malaysia Sarawak") (UNIMAS) chancellory building

Education in Malaysia falls under the remit of two federal ministries; the [Malaysian Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Education (Malaysia)") is responsible for primary and secondary education,[[278]](#cite_note-Sarawak_education-284) while the [Ministry of Higher Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Higher_Education_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Higher Education (Malaysia)") has oversight over public universities, polytechnic and community colleges.[[279]](#cite_note-285) Early childhood education is not directly controlled by the Ministry of Education as it does with primary and secondary education. However, the ministry does oversee the licensing of private kindergartens, the main form of early childhood education, in accordance with the National Pre-School Quality Standard, which was launched in 2013.[[280]](#cite_note-286)

Around the time of Federation, overall literacy in Sarawak was quite low. In 1960, the overall literacy rate was 25%, with a heavy slant in the literacy rate towards the Chinese population, 53%, compared with that of indigenous peoples which was substantially lower, only 17%.[[281]](#cite_note-287) By 2007, overall literacy in adults aged 15 and over had significantly increased to 92.3% and in 2012, this had climbed to 96%.[[282]](#cite_note-288)

There were 1480 schools in Sarawak in 2014, of which 1271 were primary, 202 were secondary and 7 were vocational/technical secondary schools.[[283]](#cite_note-289) Among these are a number of schools that date from the Brooke era, including [St. Thomas's School Kuching](/wiki/SMK_St._Thomas "SMK St. Thomas") (1848), St Mary's School Kuching (1848), and St Joseph's School Kuching (1882).[[284]](#cite_note-290) As well as government schools, there are four international schools: Tunku Putra School, a primary and secondary school offering national and Cambridge curricula, Lodge International School, which is also open to local students and uses both the British National and Cambridge systems, Kidurong International School, which is owned by [Shell](/wiki/Royal_Dutch_Shell "Royal Dutch Shell") and offers primary education mainly to children of employees but local children may enter depending on space availability, and Tenby International School, which opened in 2014 and is open to both local and expatriate children.[[285]](#cite_note-Oxford_education-291) There are also 14 [Chinese independent secondary schools](/wiki/Chinese_independent_high_school "Chinese independent high school") in Sarawak that teach in Chinese rather than English or Malay.[[286]](#cite_note-292) Previously, only Chinese students were enrolled in these schools, but mobility of the workforce has led to increasing turnover of students as parents move to other areas for employment.[[287]](#cite_note-293)[[288]](#cite_note-294) This has led to an increasing number of [Bumiputera](/wiki/Bumiputera_(Malaysia) "Bumiputera (Malaysia)") students being enrolled in Chinese primary and preschools.[[289]](#cite_note-295)

Sarawak is home to three public universities – [Universiti Malaysia Sarawak](/wiki/Universiti_Malaysia_Sarawak "Universiti Malaysia Sarawak"), [Universiti Teknologi Mara](/wiki/Universiti_Teknologi_Mara "Universiti Teknologi Mara") at [Kota Samarahan](/wiki/Kota_Samarahan "Kota Samarahan"), and [Universiti Putra Malaysia](/wiki/Universiti_Putra_Malaysia_Bintulu_Campus "Universiti Putra Malaysia Bintulu Campus") – as well as the private [Curtin University, Malaysia](/wiki/Curtin_University,_Malaysia "Curtin University, Malaysia") and [Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus](/wiki/Swinburne_University_of_Technology_Sarawak_Campus "Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus"). The latter two are satellite campuses of [Curtin University](/wiki/Curtin_University "Curtin University") in [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth") and [Swinburne University of Technology](/wiki/Swinburne_University_of_Technology "Swinburne University of Technology") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), Australia.[[278]](#cite_note-Sarawak_education-284)

With the establishment of SCORE and the associated potential of 1.6 million more jobs by 2030,[[285]](#cite_note-Oxford_education-291) the state government allocated RM1 billion from 2016 to 2020 to a Skills Development Fund for vocational education.[[290]](#cite_note-296) In 2015, [Petronas](/wiki/Petronas "Petronas") provided vocational scholarships to 150 underprivileged Sarawak students as part of its Vocational Institution Sponsorship and Training Assistance program,[[291]](#cite_note-297) although it had been criticised for under-representing local students in its previous allocations;[[292]](#cite_note-298) the company also provided support to other Sarawak vocational education centres.[[293]](#cite_note-299)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Demographics")]

The 2020 census of Malaysia reported a population of 2,453,677 in Sarawak,[[8]](#cite_note-Malaysia_census_2020-8) making it the fifth most populous state.[[294]](#cite_note-State_Planning_Unit,_Sarawak-2017-300) However, this population is distributed over a large area resulting in Sarawak having the lowest population density in the country with only 20 people per km2. The average population growth rate of 1.8%, from 2000 to 2010, is very close to the national average of 2.0%.[[134]](#cite_note-State_Planning_Unit_stats-140) In 2014, 58% of the population resided in urban areas with the remainder in rural areas, but over the next 10 years it is predicted that the urban population would rise to 65%.[[295]](#cite_note-301) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit), the [crude birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") in Sarawak was 16.3 per 1000 individuals, the [crude death rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") was 4.3 per 1000 population, and the [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") was 6.5 per 1000 live births.[[296]](#cite_note-302)

Urban populations consist predominantly of [Malays](/wiki/Malaysian_Malays "Malaysian Malays"), Melanaus, [Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"), and a small population of urban Ibans and [Bidayuhs](/wiki/Bidayuh "Bidayuh") who migrated from their home villages seeking employment.[[297]](#cite_note-Sarawak_Convention_Bureau-303) The latter two are among the more than 40 [sub-ethnic groups](/wiki/Sub-ethnic_groups "Sub-ethnic groups") of Sarawak, many of whom still inhabit remote areas and are referred to as [Orang Asal](/wiki/Orang_Asal "Orang Asal").[[298]](#cite_note-304) The Orang Asal, and Malays, of Peninsular Malaysia, Sarawak and Sabah are referred to collectively as [Bumiputera](/wiki/Bumiputera_(Malaysia) "Bumiputera (Malaysia)") (son of the soil). This classification grants them special privileges in education, jobs, finance, and political positions.[[299]](#cite_note-305)

The registration for, and issuing of, national identity cards, a legally required document for accessing various services, to these remote tribes has been problematic for many years,[[300]](#cite_note-306) and in the past had even resulted in a large number of people from the Penan ethnic group being rendered effectively stateless.[[301]](#cite_note-307) In recent years, this issue has seen progressive improvement with the implementation of systems such as mobile registration units.[[302]](#cite_note-308)

Sarawak has a large immigrant work force with as many as 150,000 registered foreign migrant workers working as domestic workers or in plantation, manufacturing, construction, services and agriculture.[[303]](#cite_note-309) However, this population of legally registered workers is overshadowed by a large population of between 320,000 and 350,000 illegal workers.[[304]](#cite_note-310)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| an Iban warrior in his traditional dress | Melanau girls with the traditional Baju Kurung |
| a Bidayuh girl | Sarawakian Chinese woman in her traditional dress of Cheongsam |

Sarawak has six major ethnic groups, [Iban](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people"), [Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"), [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), [Bidayuh](/wiki/Bidayuh "Bidayuh"), [Melanau](/wiki/Melanau_people "Melanau people"), and [Orang Ulu](/wiki/Orang_Ulu "Orang Ulu"),[[297]](#cite_note-Sarawak_Convention_Bureau-303) as well as a number of ethnic groups with smaller but still substantial populations, such as the [Kedayan](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people"), [Bugis](/wiki/Bugis "Bugis"), [Murut](/wiki/Murut_people "Murut people"), and [Indian](/wiki/Malaysian_Indian "Malaysian Indian").[[305]](#cite_note-311) In 2015, the Bidayuh and Iban, both indigenous ethnic groups of Sarawak, were officially recognised by the government of Malaysia as comprising the [Dayak people](/wiki/Dayak_people "Dayak people").[[306]](#cite_note-312) There are more than 50 tribes still existing or extinct in Sarawak but only the major tribes are listed in the Malaysian Federal Constitution.[[307]](#cite_note-313)

The population of 1,389,926 of the [Iban people](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people") in Sarawak, based on 2022 statistics, makes it the largest ethnic group in the state.[[294]](#cite_note-State_Planning_Unit,_Sarawak-2017-300) The Iban were, in the past, a society that paid particular attention to social status, especially to those who displayed martial prowess as well as to those who demonstrated expertise in various fields such as farming and oratory. Specific terms were used to refer to those who belonged to particular social strata, such as the *raja berani* (rich and the brave), *orang mayuh* (ordinary people), and *ulun* (slaves).[[308]](#cite_note-314) Despite modern influences, Iban still observe many of their traditional rituals such as Gawai Antu ([festival of the dead](/wiki/Festival_of_the_dead "Festival of the dead")) and [Gawai Dayak](/wiki/Gawai_Dayak "Gawai Dayak") ([Harvest Festival](/wiki/Harvest_Festival "Harvest Festival")).[[309]](#cite_note-315)

Although the presence of Chinese in Sarawak dates back to the 6th century AD when traders first came to the state, the [Chinese population](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") today largely consists of communities originating from immigrants during the Brooke era.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154) This migration was driven by the employment opportunities at gold mines in Bau.
Sarawak Chinese are primarily Buddhist and Christian,[[310]](#cite_note-Borneotrilogy-316) and speak a multitude of southern Chinese languages: [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people"), [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou_dialect "Fuzhou dialect"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese"), [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), and Henghua ([Putian people](/wiki/Putian_people "Putian people")), in addition to Mandarin. They celebrate major cultural festivals such as [Hungry Ghost](/wiki/Hungry_Ghost "Hungry Ghost") Festival and the [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") much as their ancestors did.[[311]](#cite_note-STF-317) Chinese settlers in Sarawak were not limited to any one area. Those who settled in Kuching did so near the Sarawak River in an area that is now referred to as [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Kuching "Chinatown, Kuching").[[312]](#cite_note-Tourism_Chinese-318) Fuzhounese immigrants from [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou"), [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), led by [Wong Nai Siong](/wiki/Wong_Nai_Siong "Wong Nai Siong") in 1901, settled along the Rajang River in what is now [Sibu](/wiki/Sibu "Sibu"), as due to [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion"),[[313]](#cite_note-319) while those who arrived in Miri sought work in the coal mines and oilfields.[[312]](#cite_note-Tourism_Chinese-318)

During the Brooke era, [Sarawak Malays](/wiki/Sarawak_Malay "Sarawak Malay") were predominantly fishermen,[[311]](#cite_note-STF-317) leading to their villages being concentrated along river banks. However, with the advent of urban development, many Malays have migrated to seek employment in public and private sectors. Traditionally, they are known for their silver and brass crafts, wood carvings, and textiles.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)[[314]](#cite_note-320)

The [Melanau](/wiki/Melanau "Melanau") are a native people of Sarawak that lived in areas primarily around the modern city of Mukah, where they worked as fishermen and craftsmen as well renowned boatbuilders. Historically the Melanau practised [Animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), a belief that spirits inhabited objects in their environment, and while this is still practised today, most Melanau have since been converted to Christianity and Islam.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)[[98]](#cite_note-Ohiopress-100)[[note 7]](#cite_note-321)[[315]](#cite_note-322)

The [Bidayuh](/wiki/Bidayuh "Bidayuh") are a southern Sarawak people,[[316]](#cite_note-323) that were referred to by early European settlers as Land Dayaks because they traditionally live on steep limestone mountains.[[317]](#cite_note-324) They account for 7.3 per cent of the population of Sarawak and are the second most numerous of the indigenous Dayak people, after the Iban. The Bidayuh are indigenous to the areas that comprise the modern day divisions of Kuching and Serian. Their language comprises five major dialects: [Biatah](/wiki/Biatah_language "Biatah language") (Padawan, Penrissen and Kuching areas), Bau, formerly known as [Jagoi](/wiki/Jagoi_language "Jagoi language") or Bau-Jagoi (Bau district), Serian, formerly called [Bukar-Sadong](/wiki/Bukar%E2%80%93Sadong_language "Bukar–Sadong language") (Serian
district), and Salako and Rara (Lundu district). All these dialects are not mutually intelligible
due to insufficient [lexicostatistical](/wiki/Lexicostatistics "Lexicostatistics") overlap between them for developing one standard
Bidayuh with a unified [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography").[[318]](#cite_note-325) Since there is no common standardised Bidayuh language, the [sociolinguistic](/wiki/Sociolinguistic "Sociolinguistic") norms of the younger generation of Bidayuhs today are influenced by [Malaysian language](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language"), with both [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Sarawak Malay](/wiki/Sarawak_Malay "Sarawak Malay") also being the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca").[[319]](#cite_note-326) Like many other indigenous peoples, the majority of the Bidayuh have been converted to Christianity,[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)
but still live in villages consisting of longhouses, with the addition of the distinctive round *baruk* where communal gatherings were held.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)

The numerous tribes who reside in Sarawak's interior such as the Kenyah, Kayan, [Lun Bawang](/wiki/Lun_Bawang "Lun Bawang"), [Kelabit](/wiki/Kelabit_people "Kelabit people"), Penan, [Bisaya](/wiki/Bisaya_(Borneo) "Bisaya (Borneo)"), and Berawan are collectively referred to as [Orang Ulu](/wiki/Orang_Ulu "Orang Ulu"). In the [Iban language](/wiki/Iban_language "Iban language"), this name means "Upriver People", reflecting the location these tribes settled in;[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154) most of them reside near the drainage basin of the Baram River.[[320]](#cite_note-Orang_Ulu_tourism-327) Both woodworking and artistry are highly visible aspects of Orang Ulu culture exemplified by mural covered longhouses, carved wooden boats, and tattooing.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154) Well-known musical instruments from the Orang Ulu are the Kayans' [sapeh](/wiki/Sapeh "Sapeh") and Kenyah's sampe' and Lun Bawang's bamboo band.[[320]](#cite_note-Orang_Ulu_tourism-327) The Kelabit and Lun Bawang people are known for their production of fragrant rice.[[321]](#cite_note-Anisia_J_Sang-328)[[320]](#cite_note-Orang_Ulu_tourism-327) As with the many other indigenous peoples of Sarawak, the majority of Orang Ulu are Christians.[[148]](#cite_note-Welman-154)

The Indians, predominantly the [Tamils](/wiki/Malaysian_Tamils "Malaysian Tamils") were brought by the British Government to work in estates as labourers and clerks.[[322]](#cite_note-Indians_in_Sarawak-329) Besides, the [Malayalee](/wiki/Malaysian_Malayalees "Malaysian Malayalees") community too exist in the cities in Sarawak.[[323]](#cite_note-330) Today, many among the Indians are known to be employed as professionals (mainly doctors) in Sarawak.[[324]](#cite_note-331)

### Languages

English was the official language of Sarawak from 1963 to 1974 due to opposition from First Chief Minister of Sarawak Stephen Kalong Ningkan to the use of the [Malaysian language](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") in Sarawak.[[325]](#cite_note-Ibanmalaysian-332) In 1974, the new Chief Minister Abdul Rahman Ya'kub recognised [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") alongside English as an official language of Sarawak.[[113]](#cite_note-Faisal-118)[[note 8]](#cite_note-333) This new status given to the Malay language was further reinforced by new education standards transitioning curriculum to Malay.[[326]](#cite_note-334) In 1985 English lost the status of an official language, leaving only Malay.[[325]](#cite_note-Ibanmalaysian-332)[[note 9]](#cite_note-335) Despite official policy, Sarawak opposition members argue that English remained the *de facto* official language of Sarawak.[[327]](#cite_note-336) English is still spoken in the legal courts, and state legislative assembly.[[328]](#cite_note-337)[[329]](#cite_note-338) In 2015, Chief Minister Adenan Satem reinstated English as an official language.[[330]](#cite_note-339)[[331]](#cite_note-340)[[332]](#cite_note-341) Sarawak's language autonomy does not extend to the educational system, with the language syllabus controlled by the federal government.[[333]](#cite_note-342)

Although the official form of Malay, [Bahasa Malaysia](/wiki/Bahasa_Malaysia "Bahasa Malaysia"), is spoken by the government administration, it is used infrequently in colloquial conversation. The local dialect of [*Bahasa Sarawak* (Sarawak Malay)](/wiki/Sarawak_Malay "Sarawak Malay") dominates the vernacular. Bahasa Sarawak is the most common language of Sarawak Malays and other indigenous tribes. The Iban language, which has minor regional variations, is the most widely spoken native language, with 60 per cent of the Sarawak population speaking it as a first language. The Bidayuh language, with six major dialects, is spoken by 10 per cent of the population. The Orang Ulu have about 30 different language dialects. While the ethnic Chinese originate from a variety of backgrounds and speak many different Southern Chinese languages such as Cantonese, Hokkien, Hakka, Fuzhou, and Teochew, they also converse in [Malaysian Mandarin](/wiki/Malaysian_Mandarin "Malaysian Mandarin").[[334]](#cite_note-343) [Tamil language](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") is spoken by the Indians in Sarawak.[[322]](#cite_note-Indians_in_Sarawak-329)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religion")]

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the largest religion in Sarawak, representing 50.1% of the total population according to the 2020 census.[[8]](#cite_note-Malaysia_census_2020-8) This makes Sarawak the only Malaysian state with a Christian majority. The earliest Christian missionaries in Sarawak were [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") ([Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans")) in 1848, followed by [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") a few years later, and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists") in 1903. Evangelization first took place among the Chinese immigrants before spreading to indigenous animists.[[335]](#cite_note-344) Other Christian denominations in Sarawak are [Borneo Evangelical Mission](/wiki/Borneo_Evangelical_Mission "Borneo Evangelical Mission") (or [Sidang Injil Borneo](/wiki/Sidang_Injil_Borneo "Sidang Injil Borneo")),[[336]](#cite_note-345) and [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists").[[337]](#cite_note-346) Indigenous people such as the Iban, Bidayuh, and Orang Ulu have adopted Christianity, although they do retain some of their traditional religious rites. Many Muslims come from the Malay and Melanau. [Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), and [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion_in_Southeast_Asia "Chinese folk religion in Southeast Asia") are predominantly practised by [Chinese Malaysians](/wiki/Chinese_Malaysians "Chinese Malaysians").[[338]](#cite_note-347) Other minor religions in Sarawak are the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"),[[339]](#cite_note-348) [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"),[[340]](#cite_note-349) [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"),[[341]](#cite_note-350) and [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism").[[342]](#cite_note-351)

Although Islam is the official religion of Malaysia, Sarawak has no official state religion.[[343]](#cite_note-352) However, during the chieftainship of Abdul Rahman Ya'kub, the Constitution of Sarawak was amended to make the Yang di-Pertuan Agong the head of Islam in Sarawak and empower the state assembly to pass laws regarding Islamic affairs. With such provisions, Islamic policies can be formulated in Sarawak and the establishment of Islamic state agencies is possible. The 1978 Majlis Islam Bill enabled the setting up of [Syariah Courts](/wiki/Syariah_Court "Syariah Court") in Sarawak with jurisdictions over matrimonial, child custody, [betrothal](/wiki/Engagement "Engagement"), inheritance, and criminal cases in the state. An appeals court and Courts of Kadi were also formed.[[113]](#cite_note-Faisal-118)[[note 10]](#cite_note-353)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Sarawak](/wiki/Culture_of_Sarawak "Culture of Sarawak")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Sape_Player%2C_Kayan_Tribe%2C_Sarawak_%287246809770%29.jpg/250px-Sape_Player%2C_Kayan_Tribe%2C_Sarawak_%287246809770%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sape_Player,_Kayan_Tribe,_Sarawak_(7246809770).jpg)

A [Kayan](/wiki/Kayan_(Borneo) "Kayan (Borneo)") tribesman, playing the [sapé](/wiki/Sapeh "Sapeh")

The location and history of Sarawak has resulted in a broad diversity of ethnicity, culture and languages. Among the indigenous peoples of Sarawak, outside influences have led to many changes over time. The Iban tribal culture in Sarawak centred on the concept of the warrior and the ability to take heads from other tribes in battle. This practice, central as it was to the Iban people, was made illegal under James Brooke's rule and ultimately faded away although reminders of the practice are still seen in some long houses.[[344]](#cite_note-354) Two other tribal peoples of the Sarawak Highlands, the Kelabit and Lun Bawang, have seen fundamental changes to their ethnic identities as a direct result of their conversion to Christianity. One major change was the shift in the focal point of their social interactions from the traditional long house to the local church. Their religious devotion has also helped shape their worldview outside of their village, particularly in response to change.[[345]](#cite_note-355) For the [Penan people](/wiki/Penan_people "Penan people"), one of the last tribes to still be practising a nomadic lifestyle within the jungle, outside influence, particularly education, has resulted in a significant decline in the population that practice the nomadic lifestyle.[[346]](#cite_note-356) Others settle down after intermixing with members of different tribes, such as the Orang Ulu.[[347]](#cite_note-357) One direct result of this diversity in cultures, engendered by a policy of tolerance to all races, is the increasing numbers of tribal peoples marrying not only other Sarawakian tribes, but also to Chinese, Malays as well as citizens of European or American descent.[[348]](#cite_note-358)

The indigenous tribes of Sarawak traditionally used oratory to pass on their culture from one generation to the next;[[349]](#cite_note-Oral_traditions-359)[[note 11]](#cite_note-360) examples of these traditional practices include the Iban's [Ngajat](/wiki/Ngajat "Ngajat") dances,[[350]](#cite_note-361) *Renong* (Iban vocal repertory),[[351]](#cite_note-362) *Ensera* (Iban oral narratives),[[325]](#cite_note-Ibanmalaysian-332)[[note 12]](#cite_note-363) and epic storytelling by the Kayan and Kenyah.[[352]](#cite_note-364)[[353]](#cite_note-365)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Ngajat%2C_the_Iban%27s_Warrior_Dance.jpg/250px-Ngajat%2C_the_Iban%27s_Warrior_Dance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ngajat,_the_Iban%27s_Warrior_Dance.jpg)

*[Ngajat](/wiki/Ngajat "Ngajat")*, the Iban warrior dance gazetted as part of Sarawak culture

In the years before federation, the colonial government recognised that British education and indigenous culture was influencing a new generation of Iban teachers. Thus, on 15 September 1958, the [Borneo Literature Bureau](/wiki/Borneo_Literature_Bureau "Borneo Literature Bureau") was inaugurated with a charter to nurture and encourage local literature while also supporting the government in its release of documentation, particularly in technical and instructional manuscripts that were to be distributed to the indigenous peoples of Sarawak and Sabah. As well as indigenous languages, documents would also be published in English, Chinese and Malay. In 1977, the bureau came under the authority of the federal government language planning and development agency, the [Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka](/wiki/Dewan_Bahasa_dan_Pustaka "Dewan Bahasa dan Pustaka") (DBP), which advocated publication only in Malay ultimately causing the demise of fledgling indigenous literature.[[325]](#cite_note-Ibanmalaysian-332)[[note 13]](#cite_note-366)

It was a number of decades before print media began to appear in Sarawak. The *[Sarawak Gazette](/wiki/Sarawak_Gazette "Sarawak Gazette")*, published by the Brooke government, recorded a variety of news relating to economics, agriculture, anthropology, archaeology, began circulation in 1870 and continues in modern times.[[354]](#cite_note-367)
However, in the decades following federation, restrictive laws and connections to businesses have meant that the media is a largely state-owned enterprise.[[325]](#cite_note-Ibanmalaysian-332)[[note 14]](#cite_note-368) One of the earliest known text publications in Borneo, *Hikayat Panglima Nikosa* (Story of Nikosa the Warrior), was first printed in Kuching, 1876.[[355]](#cite_note-369)

There are a number of museums in Sarawak that preserve and maintain artefacts of Sarawak's culture. At the foot of Mount Santubong, Kuching, is Sarawak Cultural Village, a "living museum" that showcases the various ethnic groups carrying out traditional activities in their respective traditional houses.[[356]](#cite_note-SCVMain-370)[[357]](#cite_note-371) The [Sarawak State Museum](/wiki/Sarawak_State_Museum "Sarawak State Museum") houses a collection of artefacts such as pottery, textiles, and woodcarving tools from various ethnic tribes in Sarawak, as well as ethnographic materials of local cultures.[[358]](#cite_note-372) Orang Ulu's [sapeh](/wiki/Sapeh "Sapeh") (a dug-out guitar) is the best known traditional musical instrument in Sarawak and was played for Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") during her official visit to Sarawak in 1972.[[359]](#cite_note-373)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Sarawakian cuisine](/wiki/Sarawakian_cuisine "Sarawakian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Laksa_Sarawak.JPG/250px-Laksa_Sarawak.JPG)](/wiki/File:Laksa_Sarawak.JPG)

A bowl of Sarawak [laksa](/wiki/Laksa "Laksa")

Sarawak being home to diverse communities, has a variety of ethnically influenced cuisines and cooking styles rarely found elsewhere in Malaysia. Notable dishes in the state include Sarawak [laksa](/wiki/Laksa "Laksa"),[[360]](#cite_note-374) [kolo mee](/wiki/Kolo_mee "Kolo mee"),[[361]](#cite_note-375) and [ayam pansuh](/wiki/Ayam_pansuh "Ayam pansuh").[[362]](#cite_note-376)[[363]](#cite_note-377) The state is also known for its [Sarawak layer cake](/wiki/Sarawak_layer_cake "Sarawak layer cake") dessert.[[364]](#cite_note-378)

### Portrayal in media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Portrayal in media")]

A number of international films, documentaries, television series had made Sarawak as a [principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") location, with *[Farewell to the King](/wiki/Farewell_to_the_King "Farewell to the King")* (1989), *[The Sleeping Dictionary](/wiki/The_Sleeping_Dictionary "The Sleeping Dictionary")* (2003), and *[Edge of the World](/wiki/Edge_of_the_World_(2021_film) "Edge of the World (2021 film)")* (2021) being most notable.[[365]](#cite_note-Ong_2023-379)[[366]](#cite_note-380) Among the first locally produced movies were the Iban language film *Bejalai* that was featured in the Berlin Film Festival in 1989 and the horror film *Possessed* screened in 2006.[[365]](#cite_note-Ong_2023-379)

### Holidays and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Holidays and festivals")]

Sarawakians observe a number of holidays and festivals throughout the year.[[367]](#cite_note-381) Apart from national [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Malaysia) "Independence Day (Malaysia)") and [Malaysia Day](/wiki/Malaysia_Day "Malaysia Day") celebrations, the state also celebrates its [Independence Day](/wiki/Sarawak_Independence_Day "Sarawak Independence Day") on 22 July and the State Governor's birthday.[[368]](#cite_note-382)[[369]](#cite_note-383)[[370]](#cite_note-384) Ethnic groups also celebrate their own festivals. The open house tradition allows other ethnic groups to join in the celebrations.[[371]](#cite_note-385) Sarawak is the only state in Malaysia to declare the Gawai Dayak celebration a public holiday.[[372]](#cite_note-386)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Sports")]

Sarawak sent its own teams to participate in the [1958](/wiki/1958_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") and [1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1962_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"),[[373]](#cite_note-387) and [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"); after 1963, Sarawakians competed as part of the Malaysian team.[[374]](#cite_note-388)[[375]](#cite_note-389) Sarawak hosted the Malaysian [SUKMA Games](/wiki/SUKMA_Games "SUKMA Games") in 1990, 2016 and 2024,[[376]](#cite_note-390) and was overall champion in the 1990, 1992, 1994 and 2024 SUKMA games.[[377]](#cite_note-391)[[378]](#cite_note-392) It also hosted the Para SUKMA Games in 1996, 2016 and 2024, and was overall champion for 13 consecutive editions from 1994 until 2018, before losing the title to Sabah in the 2022 edition and regained it in the 2024 edition.[[379]](#cite_note-393)[[380]](#cite_note-394) In 2019, both Sabah and Sarawak Sports Ministries work together to establish the East Malaysia Sports Commission to facilitate the organisation of more sports programmes in the two territories including other places in the Borneo islands.[[381]](#cite_note-395) The Sarawak government also plans to make Sarawak an [e-sports](/wiki/E-sports "E-sports") hub in the region.[[382]](#cite_note-396)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sarawak&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: International relations")]

Sarawak is a sister state/province to [Fujian Province](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") in China.[[383]](#cite_note-397)
